Pull Request #13331: 📝 Explicitly check for `None` in the docs when testing query `q`

### Summary

- Use `if q is not None:` over `if q:` for explicit `None` check
- Avoid assigning when it's immediately returned

I really appreciate the highly comprehensive tutorial in the first place. And free feel to let me know if my change is not a valid concern in the web framework context (as I'm just starting to learn `fastapi`).

In this case, I think it's better to explicitly check for `None` instead of checking for truthiness, but I'm not entirely sure if `q=""` (empty string) is a valid/useful query (e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:8000/items/1234?q=`)

---

While this may be less of an issue for str, I generally think using truthiness to check for None is not ideal. Imagine a scenario where a query parameter is an integer:

```python
@app.get("/items/{item_id}")
async def get_items(item_id: str, q: int | None = None):
    if q:
        return {"item_id": item_id, "q": q}
    return {"item_id": item_id}
```

In this case `q=0` would lead to surprising behaviour.
title: 📝 Explicitly check for `None` in the docs when testing query `q`

Pull Request #12997: ✨ Replace `dict` by `Mapping` on `HTTPException.headers`

HTTP Headers cannot be represented by a `dict`, as they can have duplicate entries, as shown in the example: two `set-cookie` headers:
```python
from pprint import pprint
import fastapi

response = fastapi.Response()
response.delete_cookie("cookie1")
response.delete_cookie("cookie2")

pprint(list(response.headers.items()))
# [('content-length', '0'),
#  ('set-cookie',
#   'cookie1=""; expires=Thu, 07 Nov 2024 08:26:12 GMT; Max-Age=0; Path=/; '
#   'SameSite=lax'),
#  ('set-cookie',
#   'cookie2=""; expires=Thu, 07 Nov 2024 08:26:12 GMT; Max-Age=0; Path=/; '
#   'SameSite=lax')]

headers = fastapi.datastructures.Headers(
    raw=[(b"set-cookie", x.encode()) for x in response.headers.getlist("set-cookie")]
)

exc = fastapi.HTTPException(400, headers=headers)
# Argument of type "Headers" cannot be assigned to parameter "headers" of type "Dict[str, str] | None" in function "__init__"
#   Type "Headers" is not assignable to type "Dict[str, str] | None"
#     "Headers" is not assignable to "Dict[str, str]"
#     "Headers" is not assignable to "None"PylancereportArgumentType
```

As such type of `fastapi.HTTPException`'s `headers` field should be `typing.Mapping` instead of `dict`.

In the example I tried assigning `fastapi.datastructures.Headers` to the parameter `headers`. It worked at runtime, but pyright is screaming about wrong types in the editor and CLI.

`fastapi.Request`'s `headers` field already has the type `MutableHeaders`, which is a distant descendant of `typing.Mapping` already.

A fix has also been submitted to starlette and released as part of 0.41.3, which is why its version is also bumped.

See also:
- https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12786
- https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/2749
title: ✨ Replace `dict` by `Mapping` on `HTTPException.headers`

text:
Pull Request #12508: 🐛 Ensure that dependencies can always catch validation errors

Hello everyone,

According to the [documentation](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield/#dependencies-with-yield-and-httpexception) dependencies should be able to catch and handle (postprocess, ignore, overwrite, …) exceptions.

In fact that works for almost all cases, but not for pydantic validation errors for input parameters. It looks to me that the code block was just accidentally deindented and was placed after `async with`-block not inside.

First commit adds new tests where 2 of 6 are failing. The second fixes the issue.
title: 🐛 Ensure that dependencies can always catch validation errors

text:
Discussion on PR #12508 (🐛 Ensure that dependencies can always catch validation errors):

[anton-ryzhov]: Any feedback would be highly appreciated

[anton-ryzhov]: A kind reminder 😉

[anton-ryzhov]: @tiangolo could you please take a look?

[anton-ryzhov]: I'd like to have this bug fixed. Is there a way I can help you with this PR?

[svlandeg]: Hi @anton-ryzhov,

Thanks for your contribution! Definitely appreciate the detailed explanation and the various tests you've added to the PR.

We're currently working through a backlog of PRs and will put this in the queue to review. We'll get back to you once someone in the team has been able to review this in more detail. Thanks for your patience! 🙏

[anton-ryzhov]: > Simplified test that shows the idea of this PR:

Yeah, that's exactly the problem I'm trying to solve here.

> I don't think that in current implementation "code block was just accidentally deindented and was placed after async with-block not inside" (<= quote from the initial comment). For me it looks like it was designed this way intentionally.

That was just my assumption. I think this is not very important now.

> UPD: here is the [commit](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/11191/commits/59deab57d3f3bd8b1e19bdb2724ad93ef282a366) that introduced the if not errors: block

This block was originally added [here](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/commit/a4aa79e0b4cacc6b428d415d04d234a8c77af9d5#diff-4d573079004a9f3d148baa4658e68e82b8a3d1a95d603fee8177daa92cf65c93R287-R291) and `RequestValidationError` was raised within the async stack.

And [this commit](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/11191/commits/59deab57d3f3bd8b1e19bdb2724ad93ef282a366) has put it out for some reason.

[anton-ryzhov]: What's next? Are there any chances of this to be merged?

[svlandeg]: @anton-ryzhov: we have put this on Sebastián's internal board to solicit feedback from him. Thanks for your patience 🙏

[github-actions[bot]]: This pull request has a merge conflict that needs to be resolved.

[anton-ryzhov]: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14099 has fixed the original issue. Do you still want to merge new tests for that case?

[tiangolo]: Thanks for the effort @anton-ryzhov! :cake: 

If the original issue is now solved, then I think we can close this one. Thanks for reporting back that as well! :coffee:
title: 🐛 Ensure that dependencies can always catch validation errors

Pull Request #11441: 📝 Update TrustedHostMiddleware Documentation

# Summary
This PR updates the documentation for `TrustedHostMiddleware` to reflect the current code more accurately.

## Changes
1. TrustedHostMiddleware:
- Added documentation for the www_redirect parameter. When set to True, this option redirects requests to non-www versions of allowed hosts to their www counterparts. This parameter defaults to True.

# Related PR
https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/2572 (Merged)
title: 📝 Update TrustedHostMiddleware Documentation

Pull Request #11266: ⬆️ Upgrade Starlette to >=0.37.2,<0.38.0, remove Starlette filterwarning for internal tests

Upgrading Starlette should solve
```
DeprecationWarning: The 'app' shortcut is now deprecated. Use the explicit style 'transport=WSGITransport(app=...)' instead.                                                                                                               
```
When running tests using TestClient

Also, removed filterwarning for method parameter. That was removed on Starlette [0.35.1](https://github.com/encode/starlette/releases/tag/0.35.1)
title: ⬆️ Upgrade Starlette to >=0.37.2,<0.38.0, remove Starlette filterwarning for internal tests

text:
Discussion on PR #11266 (⬆️ Upgrade Starlette to >=0.37.2,<0.38.0, remove Starlette filterwarning for internal tests):

[flying-sheep]: should be 0.37.1 because of a bug in starlette 0.37.0 I think.

[ar090]: Thanks for putting this together! Would love to see this merged as it also fixes the issue discussed [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/11019)

[ar090]: I noticed theres a few other PR's sprouting up that are attempting to make similar changes:
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/11315
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/11320
@alfreedom any chance you would be available to ✅ this one? 🙏

[alfreedom]: > I noticed theres a few other PR's sprouting up that are attempting to make similar changes: #11315 #11320 @alfreedom any chance you would be available to ✅ this one? 🙏

Approved, sorry for the delay.

[ar090]: thanks for the help here @alfreedom! @nothielf are we good to merge or waiting on anything else? Sorry to keep bumping this, we are being forced to upgrade for a vulnerability that comes with deadlines from compliance.

[nothielf]: i dont have write access to merge. but it seems is good to go  :)

[nothielf]: @alfreedom  are you able to merge it?

[alfreedom]: > @alfreedom are you able to merge it?

Nope, just maintainers have write access, maybe @alejsdev

[ar090]: @Kludex sorry to bug you on this but is this something you can merge by chance? Not sure who has the ability to merge for FastAPI

[Kludex]: Only @tiangolo .

[andgoldin]: I would like to see this get merged soon as well. Like others, I'm trying to update FastAPI to resolve [CVE-2024-24762](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-2jv5-9r88-3w3p), but am running into the `starlette` issue discussed in #11019 when trying to do so.

[Kludex]: You can just bump Python-multipart.

[aswanidutt87]: > Only @tiangolo .

@tiangolo , please merge this change as we are stuck with a CVE and are keep skipping it from sometime now.
Thank you.

[tiangolo]: Great, thanks for the contribution @nothielf! :rocket: 

And thanks all for the comments. :coffee: 

This will be available in FastAPI `0.110.1`, released in the next hours. :tada:

[alfreedom]: Change to `0.37.2,<0.38.0`, 0.37.2 version was released 2 weeks ago.

[nothielf]: done!

[Kludex]: This should be good?
```suggestion
    "starlette>=0.36.3,<0.38.0",
```

[tvasenin]: No, minimal version should NOT be less than `0.37.2`, as it fixes `DeprecationWarning` described in the first post.
Also, `0.37.1` fixes another, very important regression: see #11320.
title: ⬆️ Upgrade Starlette to >=0.37.2,<0.38.0, remove Starlette filterwarning for internal tests

text:
Pull Request #9640: Add support for new Starlette request.form() parameters max_files

Context:

- Starlette added `max_files` to `request.form()` parameters and a default limit to limiting max files. See the changes [here](https://github.com/encode/starlette/commit/8c74c2c8dba7030154f8af18e016136bea1938fa) due to [this](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-74m5-2c7w-9w3x) advisory
- This means when sending multipart-form requests to FastAPI with more than 1000 files will break, see the reported issue [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9634)
- This PR adds an option to configure this setting through FastAPI

Changes:

- Added an option `max_files` to the route decorators to set this

```python
@app.post("/", max_files=2000)
def files(files: list[UploadFile]):
    return files
```

Tests:

- Added a test to check the default behaviour remains the same and also checks that the new setting works

Questions and TODOs:

- [ ] Is this the right approach? Is there a standard FastAPI pattern for setting this type of configuration?
- [ ] Update docs if this is the right approach
- [ ] Include max_fields option
- [ ] Make test assert the error detail as well
title: Add support for new Starlette request.form() parameters max_files

Usage would be `async def endoint(files: Annotated[list[UploadFile], MaxLen(10)])` which is nicely analogous to `async def endpoint(users: Annotated[list[User], MaxLen(10)])`

> 
> Usage would be `async def endoint(files: Annotated[list[UploadFile], MaxLen(10)])` which is nicely analogous to `async def endpoint(users: Annotated[list[User], MaxLen(10)])`

@adriangb Interesting! Will take a look at that approach 

From what I can see I believe this would be the first usage of the [annotated-types](https://github.com/annotated-types/annotated-types) library in FastAPI - I would feel hesitant to put forward such a dependency without it being the recommended approach

@adriangb Ah great, that's good to know thanks! 

Do you know at all if FastAPI will start using them as a common pattern?

Pull Request #5823: 🐛 Preserve traceback when an exception is raised in sync dependency with `yield`

There is a missing of traceback when an exception raised in contextmanager_in_threadpool
Happens on Python 3.11 

I noticed the exception.traceback gets flushed after exiting the context manager.

There is an issue is opened #5740 

Edit by @Kludex : 
- Closes https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/13022
title: 🐛 Preserve traceback when an exception is raised in sync dependency with `yield`

We didn't upgrade to 3.11 because of this.

[sombek]: @iudeen @yezz123 Usually, how long it takes you guys to merge a PR?

[yezz123]: > @iudeen @yezz123 Usually, how long it takes you guys to merge a PR?

Mainly @tiangolo will review it after adding a flag of `awaiting review` so just wait it's will be reviewed asap

[github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit efd395c70c6c1fbe913635e55b420bc80b4bdf14 at: https://63bab4ceb11ed37e88ef871e--fastapi.netlify.app

[suzaku]: I patched your change with the following code and now the traceback's back. Thanks.

```python
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager as asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncGenerator, ContextManager, TypeVar

import anyio
from anyio import CapacityLimiter
from starlette.concurrency import run_in_threadpool as run_in_threadpool  # noqa

_T = TypeVar("_T")

# 
@asynccontextmanager
async def contextmanager_in_threadpool(
    cm: ContextManager[_T],
) -> AsyncGenerator[_T, None]:
    # blocking __exit__ from running waiting on a free thread
    # can create race conditions/deadlocks if the context manager itself
    # has it's own internal pool (e.g. a database connection pool)
    # to avoid this we let __exit__ run without a capacity limit
    # since we're creating a new limiter for each call, any non-zero limit
    # works (1 is arbitrary)
    exit_limiter = CapacityLimiter(1)
    try:
        yield await run_in_threadpool(cm.__enter__)
    except Exception as e:
        traceback_ = e.__traceback__
        ok = bool(
            await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(
                cm.__exit__, type(e), e, None, limiter=exit_limiter
            )
        )
        if not ok:
            if e.__traceback__ is None:
                e.__traceback__ = traceback_
            raise e
    else:
        await anyio.to_thread.run_sync(
            cm.__exit__, None, None, None, limiter=exit_limiter
        )

# Run this before starting anything FastAPI related
from fastapi.dependencies import utils
utils.contextmanager_in_threadpool = contextmanager_in_threadpool
```

[github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit 45317a3b6ba4504173ce042343df6b67a64783b1 at: https://63ccf7d40fb8da632502fbec--fastapi.netlify.app

[sombek]: @tiangolo Any idea when can we merge this?

[github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit 11fd1ce63d38de74bd87ddbb0c29ba1578d82dc1 at: https://63eb512c20977663c11a0846--fastapi.netlify.app

text:
Pull Request #4794: ♻️ Refactor `include_router` to mount sub-routers

This is a work in progress. :construction: 

## Description

Currently when a FastAPI app or a router uses `router.include_router(some_sub_router)` each of the routes in `some_sub_router` is re-created in `router`, so it is recreated in the parent router.

The intention of this PR is to mount sub-routers using Starlette's regular mount.

This would allow several things:

* Use custom router classes.
* Use custom route classes.
* Add dependencies to the router that could be executed even if there's no matching route, for example for logging.
* It would allow adding middleware per router, which currently isn't supported, not even by Starlette.
* It could possibly allow a future refactor to include a router at the top of the file before adding routes to it, currently using `.include_router()` requires calling it after all the routes are added, at the end of the file, or at another file after importing it (but this is still not possible in this PR).

## Alternative implementations and problems

There are several (too many) particular corner cases and exotic use cases that are currently supported by FastAPI, which makes this refactor/implementation non-trivial when trying to preserve compatibility for everything currently supported.

For that, I considered several approaches, but most of them have drawbacks and caveats.

## General Caveats

Adding dependencies to a router means that it could require additional data from a request, e.g. more headers, or additional body fields. And when generating the OpenAPI for a specific route, it would need to have access to all that to generate the schema properly.

At the same time, ideally, the body should be validated/parsed by pydantic once, in a single point, and collect all the validation errors. So the body field should be generated once with all the fields, it wouldn't be great if the router with its dependencies has some body field and/or some additional request requirements, validates them, returns an early error, but doesn't include any other validation errors from the actual route.

Right now, for each route, a pydantic *field* is created for each route with a body. It's a pydantic field and not a pydantic model because a field can also contain a `List[X]` (a JSON array), not only JSON objects. But that field is created once when the route is created.

All this is complicated by the fact that in pure Starlette each route doesn't have knowledge of its router, and in each router it doesn't have knowledge of any mount, or parent router. This information is (or could be) available at runtime from the ASGI scope, per request, but it would be ideal to build/compile the pydantic model once at the beginning, when starting the app, not every time there's a request.

## Features to support

The idea is to be able to support the features described above. But on top of that, ideally, to support currently supported corner-case features, like:

* Define a router prefix when creating the router (e.g. `APIRouter(prefix="/items")`).
* Define a router prefix when including it (e.g. `router.include_router(sub_router, prefix="/items")`).
* Support empty paths, as described in: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/414, to support a router with prefix `/items` that has a path `/items` (instead of only supporting `/items/` with the trailing slash)

```Python
router = APIRouter(prefix="/items")

@router.get("")
def get_all_items():
    return []
```

**Note**: this particular feature that is currently supported makes using the current Starlette mounts quite problematic, and that's where the current failing tests in this PR are.
* Support including a router more than once under different prefixes. For example, here the router is used at `/items` and `/v2/items`:

```Python
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

router = APIRouter()


@router.get("/")
def get_all_items():
    return []


@router.get("/{item_id}")
def get_item(item_id: int):
    return item_id


app.include_router(router, prefix="/items")
app.include_router(router, prefix="/v2/items")
```

* Support including a router more than once, under different prefixes **with different custom dependencies, tags, etc**:

```Python
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, FastAPI, Header

app = FastAPI()

router = APIRouter()

def new_auth(x_header: str = Header(...)):
    return {"x_header": x_header}


@router.get("/")
def get_all_items():
    return []


@router.get("/{item_id}")
def get_item(item_id: int):
    return item_id


app.include_router(router, prefix="/items")
app.include_router(router, prefix="/v2/items", dependencies=[Depends(new_auth)])
```

* Support including a router without a prefix. That would make it live in the top level, with any other routes, without concluding them. For example, two routers could share the same path `/items/`, one with a dependency and one without. This would not be supported by standard Starlette mounting.

```Python
from typing import List
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, FastAPI, Header

app = FastAPI()


def new_auth(x_header: str = Header(...)):
    return {"x_header": x_header}


protected_router = APIRouter(dependencies=[Depends(new_auth)])
simple_router = APIRouter()


@simple_router.get("/items/")
def get_all_items():
    return []


@protected_router.post("/items/")
def create_items(item_ids: List[int]):
    return item_ids


app.include_router(simple_router)
app.include_router(protected_router)
```

## Possible implementations and parts of them

## Recreate each route

Recreate each route and add it to the current router when using `router.include_router()` instead of mounting, this is the current behavior.

### Problems

* Doesn't support custom classes for routers and routes, as it creates new ones
* Doesn't support router dependencies, each dependency in the router is actually just added to the route dependencies
* Doesn't support router middleware (more precisely, doesn't support adding this as a feature), as the router itself is never called, the routes are called directly

## Mount each router

Mount the router, add an attribute to each route with the `.router` it belongs to. And add an attribute to each router with the `.parent_router`. That would allow finding the the parent dependencies and other metadata for each route.

### Problems

* This doesn't support including a router more than once as the route is strongly attached to one single chain of routers, so including it multiple times would override any other parent router's dependencies.
* It doesn't support multiple routers sharing the same path (prefix).
* It doesn't support routers that handle things at the root without trailing slash. E.g. `APIRouter(prefix="/items")` can't have a route `/items`, it can only have `/items/`.
* Extracting path parameters set in the prefix for OpenAPI could be cumbersome or not properly supported.

## Call dependencies in router

One of the objectives is to be able to have dependencies per router that could be run for anything within that router's path prefix, even if there's no matching route, e.g. for logging. An option would be to call them in the router, assuming the router is mounted and called as an ASGI app (contrary to the current behavior of only copying the router's routes).

### Problems

By default this wouldn't support extracting body parameters from those dependencies to add them to a single Pydantic body per router.

## Mount router, copy router and routes, add default router

Mount the router with standard Starlette mount, this way each router could have it's own middleware and dependencies and execute them.

But the dependencies are needed per route to extract any additional data for pydantic fields/models per route, so it would still add the dependencies to each route, and create a default route to run dependencies and middleware when there's no match.

Each router has a `.parent_router` attribute pointing to the parent router, also a method `.setup()` that reads the information that was set and the parent router's information to set its internal attributes. Then it also has a `.copy()` method that creates a new instance of the same class with the same attributes as the current router.

The copy allows setting a new parent router in the new instance and calling `.setup()` again, so that the new router can have the new parent dependencies, metadata, etc. but without overriding the old router.

Each route would also have a `.router` attribute, `.setup()` and `.copy()` method.

### Problems

* By default this wouldn't support routers with a prefix and a root route without trailing slash.
* It doesn't support multiple routers sharing the same path (prefix).

### Mount router, copy routers/routes, custom mount

This is everything above plus an internal new custom `APIMount` extending Starlette's one, with a bunch of extra logic to support root paths under a prefix without a trailing slash.

**This, plus everything above, is mostly what this PR implements**.

### Problems

* It still doesn't support redirecting in a router with a prefix ending without a trailing slash, with a root path without slash (e.g. prefix `"/items"` and path `""` to handle `/items`) and redirecting the path with trailing slash to non-trailing slash: `/items/` -> `/items`.
* It still doesn't support including multiple routers under the same prefix, once a router takes a request for a prefix there's no chance for other routers to handle it.

## Details of this PR

The way the current PR implements all this is by having the extra attributes `.router` (for routes) `.parent_router` (for rotuers) and the methods `.setup()`, `.copy()`, and having several new attributes like `._router_dependencies` to store the dependencies added on instantiation, apart from the ones including the dependencies of the parent router an the chain of parent routers that are set on the normal `.dependencies` attribute, and those are now set when calling `.setup()`.

This allows calling `.copy()` on a router or route to get a new instance with all the same attributes, and then set the new parent router, and then call `.setup()` again to set any new dependencies, metadata, etc.

### Details and Problems

This PR is too big, the code is too complex to my taste. There's too much logic setting the private attributes to then recompute the final ones with `.setup()`.

And because `.setup()` is called every time a router is included, in the end it will be called many times, although this is only on instantiation, at startup.

The whole point of storing private attributes and then setting up the rest right after/during the instantiation is to have those values cached instead of computing them live. But this means there's a lof of "caches" in attributes/memory in the code, and that's always cumbersome to handle. One of the main points of this refactor is to better support subclasses of routers and routes, but all the extra logic to store private attributes and then `.setup()` on instantiation or after including them in other routers and the logic to `.copy()` routers and routes, and then setting the new `.router` or `.parent_router` would make it more delicate and complex to implement those subclasses.

This PR also has a lot of custom code to support the corner cases. For example, there's a custom `APIMount` with quite some logic duplicated from Starlette just to support routers with a prefix and a root route without a prefix (e.g. `APIRouter(prefix="/items")` and a route inside that handles `/items`, instead of `/items/`).

## Questions

There's one existing feature that conflicts with a desired one (maybe more). Currently it is possible to include multiple routers under the same path (e.g. one with more restricted dependencies). But one of the objectives is to support middleware and dependencies per router that are executed even when there's no matching route (e.g. for logging, CORS). In this case, imagine there's:

```Python
protected_router = APIRouter(prefix="/items", dependencies=[Depends(new_auth)])
simple_router = APIRouter(prefix="/items")
```

Here we would probably want the routes in both routers to be included (as is currently the case).

But if there's a request for `/items` that doesn't match any path, should the dependency `new_auth` be executed?

If both routers define different CORS middlewares, which one should be applied?

---

I'm also wondering if I can make the implementation simpler, probably without all the private attributes and copies, maybe computing the data when needed is not that expensive or maybe I can do all that in a simpler and safer way, I'm gonna try that now.
title: ♻️ Refactor `include_router` to mount sub-routers

text:
Pull Request #4525: Reject single string in tags to avoid unexpected behavior

# Summary

Using a string as a value for parameter `tags` leads to strange results. For example `app.include_router(router, tags="test")` results in tags `t`, `e` and `s`. This PR attempts to remove this unexpected behaviors and guide developers to use lists instead.

This is an alternative to #4431, which converted a string to a list with one string.

# Motivation

I often use a single tag in my routers, which even the documentation states is very common.

When I include a router in my app, I often do the mistake of using a string instead of a list, which generates a very weird result, as seen below: 

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter

app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter()

@router.get("")
def test():
    return {"msg" : "Hello, world."}

app.include_router(router, prefix="/test", tags="test")
```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7232812/149621686-3237fac3-42b8-46de-bea9-767bc31d02bb.png)

This lead me to try implement a fix for this itch which often annoys me. I don't know how often it happens to other users, but I find it hard to see a use case for the current behavior - hence the PR.

# Description

In this PR, I assert that the parameter `tags` is not a `string`, to fail fast rather than accepting with unexpected results. See example below for the same code:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter

app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter()

@router.get("")
def test():
    return {"msg" : "Hello, world."}

app.include_router(router, prefix="/test", tags="test")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn

    uvicorn.run("test:app", host="127.0.0.1", port=8000, log_level="info", debug=True, reload=True)
```

Output:

```
$ python app3.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "E:\dev\git\3rdparty\fastapi\app3.py", line 10, in <module>
    app.include_router(router, prefix="/test",tags="test")
  File "E:\dev\git\3rdparty\fastapi\fastapi\applications.py", line 350, in include_router
    self.router.include_router(
  File "E:\dev\git\3rdparty\fastapi\fastapi\routing.py", line 674, in include_router
    assert not isinstance(tags, str), """tags should not be a string: please use ["tag"] instead of "tag"."""
AssertionError: tags should not be a string: please use ["tag"] instead of "tag".
```


I also added tests to assert this behavior.

# Request for comments

I'm a first time contributor, so comments are welcome. Specifically:
- is this a desirable improvement?
- did I miss any method with the `tags` parameter? 
- do these tests make sense?

My first approach was to convert a string to a list (see #4431), but after feedback from @AlrasheedA this seems simpler.
title: Reject single string in tags to avoid unexpected behavior

Pull Request #3527: Add tool function to reload openapi.json and re-generate dependant in route

related to #3524 

I add a function in `utils` to trigger reload openapi.json and manually re-generate `dependant` in `APIRoute`

And for doing that I change a little in `APIRoute` to make generate `dependant` as a function for reusing this function 
in my new utils function
title: Add tool function to reload openapi.json and re-generate dependant in route

text:
Discussion on PR #3527 (Add tool function to reload openapi.json and re-generate dependant in route):

[yangyaofei]: @tiangolo could you please explain this reviewer's behavior ? 

I make this PR , someone review it, and I reply and explain the code. 

Suddenly the reviewer leave this PR and give nothing to explain.  

I can understand no one start to review this PR because this is a hot project, and I can understand my PR closed with a sentence of reason. 

But I cannot understand there is a reviewer to review this PR and he just leave without any explain and further action and make this PR like a zombie.

[BlazJurisic]: any updates on this?

[yangyaofei]: @BlazJurisic I want to know too, the reviewer just leave it here, i don't know how.
If you want use it to reload, just use the function that I add in your code.

[bropuffshroom]: Does the reviewer actually read the code? 
the function in question is the insert function of a standard python list object, and the 0 is the position argument.

[reference](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html)
> list.insert(i, x)
Insert an item at a given position. The first argument is the index of the element before which to insert, so a.insert(0, x) inserts at the front of the list, and a.insert(len(a), x) is equivalent to a.append(x).

Using a variable to annotate it seems unnecessarily verbose to me. This left the PR hang for 2 years lol

This function will be handy for scenarios where routes needs to be dynamically handled at runtime.

[bropuffshroom]: Just in case anyone was looking for a simple refresh, you can actually do this to regenerate the json schema (openapi.json):

```
app = FastAPI()
app.openapi_schema = None
app.openapi()
```

Thanks for the code in the commit - it saved me some time

[yangyaofei]: @bropuffshroom well, the you code you show may not work for me(I did try that in my project). It just shellow update the `openapi.json` not work with the validation.

support there is a class:
``` python

class TypeEnum(Enum, Str):
    TYPE_A: 'A'
    TYPE_B: 'B'

class A(BaseModel):
    id: str
    type: TypeEnum

```

And if I use `aenum` to update the `TypeEnum`

```
extend_enum(TypeEnum, "TYPE_C", "C")

```

The validation of json:
```json
{
"id": "iddd",
"type": "C"
}

```
will be invalid.

---------

That's just my usecase, I think there must be some other usecase need a `deep reload`.
title: Add tool function to reload openapi.json and re-generate dependant in route

Pull Request #2606: 🐛 Fix `jsonable_encoder` using `include` and `exclude` parameters for non-Pydantic objects

This fixes #2594. I added more few cases with include and exclude parameters, which were missing. Now the parameters are consistent with the way that it works with Pydantic.

When I first added the new tests in `test_jsonable_encoder.py` (without changing the codebase), these were the errors I got:
```
================================= test session starts =================================
platform linux -- Python 3.8.5, pytest-5.4.3, py-1.10.0, pluggy-0.13.1
rootdir: /home/xaviml/work/fastapi
plugins: cov-2.10.0, asyncio-0.14.0
collected 15 items                                                                    

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py FFF............                                  [100%]

====================================== FAILURES =======================================
__________________________________ test_encode_dict ___________________________________

    def test_encode_dict():
        pet = {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
        assert jsonable_encoder(pet) == {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
>       assert jsonable_encoder(pet, include={"name"}) == {"name": "Firulais"}
E       AssertionError: assert {'name': 'Fir...name': 'Foo'}} == {'name': 'Firulais'}
E         Omitting 1 identical items, use -vv to show
E         Left contains 1 more item:
E         {'owner': {'name': 'Foo'}}
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py:99: AssertionError
__________________________________ test_encode_class __________________________________

    def test_encode_class():
        person = Person(name="Foo")
        pet = Pet(owner=person, name="Firulais")
        assert jsonable_encoder(pet) == {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
>       assert jsonable_encoder(pet, include={"name"}) == {"name": "Firulais"}
E       AssertionError: assert {'name': 'Fir...name': 'Foo'}} == {'name': 'Firulais'}
E         Omitting 1 identical items, use -vv to show
E         Left contains 1 more item:
E         {'owner': {'name': 'Foo'}}
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py:112: AssertionError
________________________________ test_encode_dictable _________________________________

    def test_encode_dictable():
        person = DictablePerson(name="Foo")
        pet = DictablePet(owner=person, name="Firulais")
        assert jsonable_encoder(pet) == {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
>       assert jsonable_encoder(pet, include={"name"}) == {"name": "Firulais"}
E       AssertionError: assert {'name': 'Fir...name': 'Foo'}} == {'name': 'Firulais'}
E         Omitting 1 identical items, use -vv to show
E         Left contains 1 more item:
E         {'owner': {'name': 'Foo'}}
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py:125: AssertionError
================================== warnings summary ===================================
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10

env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
  /home/xaviml/work/fastapi/env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10: DeprecationWarning: "@coroutine" decorator is deprecated since Python 3.8, use "async def" instead
    def run(*args, loop=None, executor=None, **kwargs):

-- Docs: https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/warnings.html
=============================== short test summary info ===============================
FAILED tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py::test_encode_dict - AssertionError: assert {'n...
FAILED tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py::test_encode_class - AssertionError: assert {'...
FAILED tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py::test_encode_dictable - AssertionError: assert...
====================== 3 failed, 12 passed, 6 warnings in 0.51s =======================
```

You can see that even I added the same tests to `test_encode_model_with_default`, they all passed. This is because the include and exclude done by Pydantic was done correctly. Therefore, what this PR adds is:
- More test cases to `include` and `exclude` parameters from the `jsonable_encoder` function
- Consistent functionality with different types of objects (BaseModel, dict, and Object)
title: 🐛 Fix `jsonable_encoder` using `include` and `exclude` parameters for non-Pydantic objects

[adriangb]: Question: does this cover the use case of CSV-like quoted strings: `GET /items?foo="a,bc",d` (example taken from [here](https://specs.openstack.org/openstack/api-wg/guidelines/pagination_filter_sort.html#filtering)). Pull Request #464: Only use default parameter values for path parameters (#450)

This change allows endpoint functions to be defined with parameters with defaults, but for those defaults to be ignored if the function parameter subsequently appears as a path parameter. The default value is only operative if the parameter is a query parameter.

This change includes a new set of tests for this functionality.
title: Only use default parameter values for path parameters (#450)

text:
Pull Request #14192: 🐛 Fix ignoring background tasks added after `yield`

This fixes a regression introduced in 0.118 whereby background tasks added _after_ a `yield` within a dependency don't get executed.

Resolves: #14137
title: 🐛 Fix ignoring background tasks added after `yield`

text:
Discussion on PR #14192 (🐛 Fix ignoring background tasks added after `yield`):

[JP-Ellis]: The issue with Python 3.12 and Pydantic should be resolved by #14186. Once that is merged, I will rebase this PR.

[JP-Ellis]: Updated the test to ensure 100% coverage

[ezeparziale]: Any update on this PR?

[JP-Ellis]: I personally don't really care which way it is done, and am happy to defer to what people might expect.

I was under the impression that the current behaviour, that is, executing pre-yield background tasks early, was intentional and therefore there was a gap in the post-yield task, which this PR addresses.

If you want to have all background tasks executed post-yield, I can certainly amend this PR to do so.

[YuriiMotov]: > If you want to have all background tasks executed post-yield, I can certainly amend this PR to do so

I personally think we should do it this way, but I haven't investigated how difficult it is to implement

[JP-Ellis]: Great! I'll take a look at doing this in the next few days (don't have time today unfortunately).

[JP-Ellis]: @YuriiMotov Looking into running all background tasks _after_, there would be some bigger changes required.

Background tasks in pre-yield dependencies get included as part of Starlette's background task handling, and get executed in [Starlette's `Response.__call__`](https://github.com/Kludex/starlette/blob/4941b4a04993d087dea66e9287fe9472babee879/starlette/responses.py#L155-L167):

```py
async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
    prefix = "websocket." if scope["type"] == "websocket" else ""
    await send(
        {
            "type": prefix + "http.response.start",
            "status": self.status_code,
            "headers": self.raw_headers,
        }
    )
    await send({"type": prefix + "http.response.body", "body": self.body})


    if self.background is not None:
        await self.background()
```

When FastAPI runs the post-yield logic, the new background tasks get added to the same list, but there's no logic to eventually handle them.

I can see two ways forward:

1. Keep the PR as is, with some background tasks handled by Starlette, and some by FastAPI. Pragmatic, although perhaps not ideal.
2. Change the way background tasks are collected in FastAPI so that they are not passed to Starlette at all. I do not know if this will result in any other side-effects though.

[YuriiMotov]: @JP-Ellis, thanks!
Then, let's wait for Sebastian to take a look and decide how we should proceed with this.

[github-actions[bot]]: This pull request has a merge conflict that needs to be resolved.
title: 🐛 Fix ignoring background tasks added after `yield`

Discussion on PR #13936 (🔨 Add Russian translations LLM prompt):

[github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit dff8b08d44be78f15238363e95189f6b9633a2c4 at: https://1f575755.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev

[YuriiMotov]: My notes (work in progress):

For the next terms, use the following translations (use correct suffixes, prefixes and endings depending on the context):
* production (meaning production software or environment): продакшн
* completion (meaning code auto-completion): автозавершение
* editor (meaning component of IDE): редактор кода
* adopt (meaning start to use): использовать (or `начать использовать`)
* headers (meaning HHTP-headers): HTTP-заголовки
* cookie sessions: сессии с использованием cookie
* tested (adjective): протестированный
* middleware: middleware (don't translate, but add `(Промежуточный слой)` if clarification is needed)
* path operation: обработчик пути
* proprietary: проприетарный
* benchmark: бенчмарк (add `(тест производительности)` if clarification is needed)
* ASGI server: ASGI-сервер
* In a hurry? : Нет времени?
* Response Status Code: статус-код ответа
* HTTP status code: HTTP статус-код
* issue (meaning GitHub issue): Issue (add `(тикет\обращение)` if clarification is needed)
* PR (meaning GitHub pull request): пулл-реквест (add `(запрос на изменение)` if clarification is needed)
* run (meaning run the code): запустить (or `прогнать` if it's about testing the program)
* to reach users: донести до пользователей (or `привлечь внимание пользователей` in the promotion context)
* body (meaning HTTP request body): тело запроса
* body parameter : body-параметр (or 'параметр тела запроса')
* validate: валидировать (or `выполнить валидацию`)
* requirements (meaning dependencies): зависимости
* auto-reload: авто-перезагрузка (or `перезагрузить автоматически` if used as a verb)
* show (meaning show on the screen): отобразить
* parsing (noun): парсинг
* origin (in web development): origin (add `(источник)` if clarification is needed)
* include: включать (add `в себя` if it's appropriate, or use `содержать` as an alternative)
* virtual environment: виртуальное окружение

Pull Request #11140: ✨ Add support for PEP695 TypeAliasType

Fixes: #10719
Discussed (by others) in: #10662

Adds support for [PEP695](https://peps.python.org/pep-0695/). 

Sidesupport (please check if intended!): Only loads `*_py312` files for python3.12 and later, as only the test introduces syntax not compatible for python < 3.12.

```python
def some_complex_function():
    return 42
    
type DependedVariable = Annotated[int, some_complex_function]
```

Using `DependedVariable` in route dependencies will now work :).
title: ✨ Add support for PEP695 TypeAliasType

text:
Pull Request #10500: 📝 Cleanup local links in docs

(I will recreate this in a later PR)

The PR only touches local links to markdowns (links to .pngs and .pys are all good) and does not touch external links.

Aside of fixing broken local links,

* it makes absolute links relative (no links anymore which start with `/`)
* it appends `index.md` where missing, so that all links either are hash-only in-document links or links with `.md` in them
* it removes `./` from start of links

The following patterns can be used to search for these links (and should find nothing with this PR applied):

* Links without `index.md`: the regex `\]\((?!http)[^)#]*/(#[^#)]+)?\)`
* Absolute links: `](/`
* Links with "./" at the start: `](./`

I also fix untranslated `#hash-parts` in links, so far in `es`, `fr`, `em`. Will add more commits. When the author has not translated the link text, I leave it, instead of guessing.

[This script](https://gist.github.com/nilslindemann/4a996ac153c7ada29b88c8b4a7bab8c2) tests if all links are according to the above definitions, and it checks if the path parts of links point to existing files. It does not check if the hash points to an existing heading. After this PR is applied, the script outputs:

```
> python linkchecker.py 

Each link under /home/nils/FastAPI/fastapi/docs is:

* a https link to an external url
* a well-formed local relative link to a .md / .png / .py

These files are referenced in links but do not exist:
    /home/nils/FastAPI/fastapi/docs/en/docs/advanced/async-sql-databases.md
```
title: 📝 Cleanup local links in docs

Pull Request #9753: ✨ Allow using dependables with `functools.partial()`

Discussion: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/9744

Using `functools.partial()` with dependables feels like a very obvious idiom. `partial()` already works for dependables that are plain function, but it doesn't work consistently, partial of async and callable classes didn't work consistently.

This PR makes partial() work consistently in all combinations.

test_dependency_class.py have been renamed to test_dependency_types.py to reflect its bigger scope now.

There's quite a bit of repetition in the tests, part of me wants to just refactor those; but another part of me also wants to keep the tests simple.
title: ✨ Allow using dependables with `functools.partial()`

[jesse-r-s-hines]: A monkey patch workaround for until this gets merged:
```python
fastapi_analyze_param = fastapi.dependencies.utils.analyze_param
def fastapi_analyze_param_monkey_patch(*, annotation, **kwargs):
    """
    Workaround for until fastapi!9315 is merged.
    This needs to be patched in before you import/call any APIRouters
    """
    from typing import Annotated, get_args, get_origin
    from copy import copy
    if get_origin(annotation) is Annotated:
        annotation = Annotated[tuple(copy(arg) for arg in get_args(annotation))]
    return fastapi_analyze_param(annotation = annotation, **kwargs)
fastapi.dependencies.utils.analyze_param = fastapi_analyze_param_monkey_patch
```

text:
Pull Request #5489: Add tutorial for ContextVar based DB session access from a single place

Database sessions can be accessed in FastAPI either through dependency or request state. Both of these methods require the db session to be passed from the controller layer to the target function. If there are more function layers in between, which is often the case for larger applications, the DB session needs to be passed through multiple layers. This can go against DRY principles. 

A similar issue regarding this was reported here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/2894

There is a better solution with `contextvars` and middlewares in this case. This PR adds the tutorial in the official doc
title: Add tutorial for ContextVar based DB session access from a single place

text:
Pull Request #4105: Cache cloning of pydantic types globally for 2x+ startup speed-up

If `response_model=...` is specified in a route handler, FastAPI creates a copy of the type to avoid accidental leakage of secrets. This is done in a pure way, but is only cached within a single route. This means that that if there's models that occur several times (for instance, multiple routes that return the same model), identical work is done and re-done. This PR change adds a cache for the conversion: when converting a type that's already been seen, just use the cache.

This dramatically improves the start-up time of the server, making for a nicer developer experience when running locally, or running tests, and reducing cold-start times if run in FaaS/serverless platforms like AWS Lambda.

For instance, we have an app with 144 routes, defined in the usual way with an `app = FastAPI(...)` in app.py and various routers across other files.

I measured the impact on this change in two ways.

- Just the time to initialise all the routes etc (command below)
- The time to reach "Application startup complete" when actually running (`uvicorn.run(..., port=8000, reload=True, debug=True, workers=1)`)

| measurement    | 0.85.0 (s) | this patch (s) |
|----------------|-----------:|---------------:|
| initialisation |       4.65 |           1.43 |
| startup complete   |       11.45 |           3.38 |

Initialisation measurement command:

    python -c 'import time; start = time.perf_counter(); import .app; print(time.perf_counter() - start)'

---

Thanks for FastAPI!
title: Cache cloning of pydantic types globally for 2x+ startup speed-up

text:
Discussion on PR #4105 (Cache cloning of pydantic types globally for 2x+ startup speed-up):

[huonw]: I tried this in our AWS Lambda infrastructure. The cold start initialisation drops from ~6s to ~2s, not including the time to actually serve the request, which is typically milliseconds. That is, 4s/two-thirds of the lambda start-up time is this repeated redundant type conversion.

(We do realise that FastAPI works great as a standalone server, not in lambda too.)

[ghandic]: Likewise LGTM, massive improvement for serverless 🚀

[huonw]: Hello @tiangolo, when you have chance, could you take a look at this PR? Thank you!
title: Cache cloning of pydantic types globally for 2x+ startup speed-up

text:
Pull Request #4094: Fix issue for SQLAlchemy Dataclasses post 0.67

*edited* due to change from flag based approach in the route to an optional property on the dataclasses.

As detailed in #3616, SQLAlchemy dataclasses don't work nicely with the new defaults introduced in FastAPI 0.67. This pull request adds the option for dataclasses to include a `read_with_orm_mode` flag, matching the options available to Pydantic models.

Currently this is specified as a special dunder property: `__read_with_orm_mode__` on each dataclass, though it could take a different form. In my codebases, all my SQLAlchemy dataclasses inherit from the same base class, so I would only be setting this property on the base class.

Besides disabling the new dataclass parsing feature in some manner, it seems as though it'd be impossible to use the SQLAlchemy dataclass pattern as described in their docs [here](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/orm/mapping_styles.html#declarative-mapping-with-dataclasses-and-attrs).
title: Fix issue for SQLAlchemy Dataclasses post 0.67

Pull Request #2473: Propagate callable's return type annotation for Depends/Security

I was struck by the following paragraph in [tutorial for `Depends`](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies/#type-annotation-vs-depends):
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/51e920e2fcf3e2a715081f28e3187549f2bf531a/docs/en/docs/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies.md#L138-L150

I though that's easily solvable with [`typing.TypeVar`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#typing.TypeVar), and this PR serves as a untested proof-of-concept, further corroborated only by the fact that the following short snippet passes mypy checks:
```py
from typing import Callable, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')

class C:
    ...

def foo(x: Callable[..., T]) ->  T:
    return bar()

def bar():
    return ...

c: C = foo(C)   # <-- no error here
```
This alleviates the need for repeating the dependency (e.g. `CommonQueryParams`) in the annotation. :crossed_fingers:
title: Propagate callable's return type annotation for Depends/Security

Pull Request #2235: multiple aliases on fields (fixes #1889)

When completed, this PR will allow fields to be defined in this way as per https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1889:

```py
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import Required
from fastapi import FastAPI, Query

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/")
def get(
        question: str = Query(Required, aliases=('q', 'question')),
        answer: Optional[int] = Query(None, aliases=('a', 'answer'))
    ):
    return {
        'question': question,
        'answer': answer,
    }


app.setup()
```

TODO:
- [x] Make backwards compatible with base pydantic models
- [x] Refactor the typing to make more sense. 
- [ ] Figure out how to communicate that a required value was not received for a field with multiple aliases.
- [ ] Update documentation.
- [ ] Add testing for MultiAliasableModelField class.
title: multiple aliases on fields (fixes #1889)

text:
Pull Request #1434: [WIP] Optionally skip json_encoder for orjson & response_model_skip_validation

This PR is to address optimizations noted in #1359 

Done:
* [x] Add a `response_model_skip_validation` parameter that is to be set on a per-request basis. This will skip validation if `response_model` is set.
* [x] Skip `jsonable_encoder` if the `Response` has `skip_jsonable_encoder = True` set and various other conditions pass.
  We set this for `orjson` because as per https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1224#issuecomment-617243856 it appears to handle many common python datatypes. We added `decimal.Decimal` as that was the only one missing.
* [x] If a `response_model` has been specified we require `response_model_skip_validation` before allowing skipping of `jsonable_encoder`, as it skips coercion.
  As the user just said they know what they are doing. Right?
* [ ] Allow skipping validation globally via configuring the app.
* [ ] Update docs

Motivation is based on benchmarks (more details at #1359) I performed.
Some conclusions are:
* Skipping `jsonable_encoder` can increase performance by over 35% (probably more if no `response_model` has been specified.
* Skipping validation can increase performance by 30-50%
* Skipping both can increase performance by 140% (https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1359#issuecomment-622998691 provides an explanation)
title: [WIP] Optionally skip json_encoder for orjson & response_model_skip_validation

text:
Pull Request #15149: ⬆️ Support free-threaded Python 3.14t

Continues from #13946: adding support for free-threaded Python 3.14t

* Extend test suite with a configuration for `python-version: "3.14t"`
* Allow the test suite to run when `orjson` and/or `ujson` is not installed, cf also https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14964 which deprecated `ORJSONResponse` & `UJSONResponse`
* Remove `orjson` & `ujson` as a standard test dependency
* Add an additional test configuration to test for deprecated `ujson`/`orjson` tests (to ensure coverage goes back to 100%)
* Ensure that `fastar` 0.9.0 is used

## Checklist

- [x] Fix issues with `orjson` & `ujson` on free-threaded run
- [x] Run `orjson` & `ujson` tests in one specific non-free-threaded run
- [x] Fix `fastar` issue on free-threaded run: https://github.com/DoctorJohn/fastar/releases/tag/v0.9.0
- [x] Fix SQLAlchemy cyextension issues on Ubuntu

## Open question

Decided not to do this:
- [ ] Bump `pydantic-core` & `strawberry-graphql` (and maybe others) to allow running `uv-resolution: lowest-direct` on 3.14t as well. This will negatively impact other users on older Python versions / older external lib versions.
title: ⬆️ Support free-threaded Python 3.14t

[github-actions[bot]]: This pull request has a merge conflict that needs to be resolved. [ngoldbaum]: Looks like the Mac and Windows tests aren't picking up fastar 0.9.0. [svlandeg]: > Looks like the Mac and Windows tests aren't picking up fastar 0.9.0. It's unclear to me why the `3.14t` test on Windows wasn't installing the latest `fastar` 0.9.0, https://github.com/fastapilabs/fastapi-cloud-cli has it at `"fastar >= 0.8.0"`. Anyway for now I explicitely require 0.9.0 and then that test does succeed 🎉 The Mac test is a different issue: I was running that one with `uv-resolution: lowest-direct` which means that it tries out the lower bounds of our supported ranges. Here, it crashed for an older version of Pydantic, which is not surprising. Now the only open question is whether we want to bump those lower bounds (which may be annoying for users on older versions of Python & external repos), or just leave them and assume that users running `3.14t` will also have updated dependencies 🤷
title: ⬆️ Support free-threaded Python 3.14t

Pull Request #13930: 🌐 Update German translations for recently updated docs (using LLMs)

🌐 Update German translations for recently updated docs (using LLMs)

This is done with the German LLM prompt file (https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13931), if there are things that are wrong in this PR, things that shouldn't be updated or things missing, it would be better to tweak that prompt file to instruct the LLM to better handle those cases than to review this PR directly.

---

These are the existing translations that have been updated in the original files in English since the last update in German.

These are probably the most delicate, because the prompt is adjusted to try both:

* Update the existing translation to cover whatever is new in the original docs in English.
* Keep what's already fine, avoid just rewriting everything every time, otherwise this could run over and over again generating something completely new each time, and any update would make the diff giant, instead of focusing on what actually changed.

Both objectives are at odds, one would expect the LLM to change more things, the other would expect the LLM to change fewer things.

There are some pages that have changed quite a bit in the original in English since they were translated, so I imagine there's a chance the LLM would end up defaulting to changing more in those files.

There are also some files that have changed only in specific sections, for example adding or removing things at the end. It seems the LLM can get these fairly well, only adding (or removing) that final section but respecting the rest.

If there are some files that are changed too much unnecessarily, or not working well, maybe a note with which files are okay to merge would work, so that I can remove the bad ones to tweak them further and keep the good translation files.
title: 🌐 Update German translations for recently updated docs (using LLMs)

Pull Request #13767: ✨ Extract descriptions of parameters and responses from docstring

This pull request implements parsing docstrings via griffe and sets the relevant openapi descriptions to the corresponding docstring part.

The openapi has 3 description fields: the route itself, each parameter and the response.
With docstrings, you can only set one: The route itself. You can set the description for parameter and response using attributes in the router or annotations.
If I describe the function with all arguments, e.g. to build a documentation via mkdocs, the arguments that get autofilled by depends will appear in the openapi spec. If I want the description in the openapi spec and in the docstring there will be redundancy. Doing it only in the fastapi part, it will not be part of mkdocs (or similar).
As described by #3225, using docstrings is complicated, because the structure is not standardized. So I solve this using griffe, which can parse the most common formats (google, numpy, sphinx). Currently i set it to google format, but the plan is to make this configurable. However, if the description is set by fastapi attributes, this will take precedence.

```python
@router.get(
    "/events/{eventId}",
    status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK,
    summary="Get event details",
)
@alog
async def get_event_details(
    auth: Annotated[Auth, Depends(authorize(AuthStrategy.HYBRID))],
    db: Annotated[AsyncSession, Depends(get_db)],
    event_id: Annotated[int | uuid.UUID, Path(alias="eventId")],
) -> AddEditGetEventResponse:
    """Retrieve details of a specific event either by its event ID, or via its UUID.

    args:
        auth: the user's authentification status
        db: the current database
        event_id: the ID or UUID of the event

    returns:
        the event details
    """
    return await _get_event_details(db, event_id, auth.user)
```

will be converted to:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/43767a4b-3594-40f6-9c61-01d0ace72613)

Before i continue to work on this, i want to know if adding a dependency is ok. If so, i would allow to set the parse format of the docstring (griffe supports google, numpy and sphinx).
title: ✨ Extract descriptions of parameters and responses from docstring

Pull Request #13628: 📝 Add docs for IoC containers using Dishka as an example

With the **dependency injection** (DI) pattern becoming increasingly common — especially due to its popularization through FastAPI — it's important to clarify when the built-in `Depends` mechanism is sufficient, and when using a third-party DI library is more appropriate.

This PR adds a new guide to the _«How To»_ section that explores the difference between **dependency injection** and **dependency inversion**, and how they relate. It combines a relatable real-life analogy with practical, detailed code examples.

I chose **Dishka** as the third-party integration example because:

* **it has strong community support and many integrations, including with FastAPI;**

* **it builds on lessons learned from earlier DI libraries;**

* **it efficiently resolves complex dependencies graphs;**

* **it fully complies with the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP);**

* **it is async- and type-oriented — just like FastAPI;**

* **it provides an extensive, flexible API ready for an almost boundless scalability even with a monolith.**

The guide is written in a friendly yet technical tone, consistent with the rest of the documentation. It includes helpful links to related sections and aims to encourage developers — including newcomers — to adopt _best practices_ around dependency management.

Special thanks for the inspiration to make this PR:

* **@Tishka17 and all the Dishka contributors;**

* **a great [article](https://habr.com/ru/articles/867040/) on DI in FastAPI (in a non-English language);**

* **a helpful [curated list](https://github.com/sfermigier/awesome-dependency-injection-in-python) of Python DI tools.**
title: 📝 Add docs for IoC containers using Dishka as an example

[YuriiMotov]: What if we simplify this part of code example:

```python
def get_city_client(settings: ClientSettingsDep) -> CityClient:
    return CityClient(settings=settings)


CityClientDep = Annotated[CityClient, Depends(get_city_client)]


def get_village_client(settings: ClientSettingsDep) -> VillageClient:
    return VillageClient(settings=settings)


VillageClientDep = Annotated[VillageClient, Depends(get_village_client)]


geo_router = APIRouter(prefix="/geo", tags=["Geo"])


@geo_router.get(
    "",
    response_class=PlainTextResponse,
    summary="Fetches geodata.",
)
async def get(client: CityClientDep) -> str:
    return await client.request()
```

to this:

```python
async def get_client(settings: ClientSettingsDep) -> Client:
    return CityClient(settings=settings)


geo_router = APIRouter(prefix="/geo", tags=["Geo"])


@geo_router.get(
    "",
    response_class=PlainTextResponse,
    summary="Fetches geodata.",
)
async def get(client: Annotated[Client, Depends(get_client)]) -> str:
    return await client.request()
```

We don't need `VillageClientDep` and `CityClientDep` at the same time. If we need to switch between them, we can just modify `return CityClient(settings=settings)` to `return VillageClient(settings=settings)`.

Now, most of the following points are irrelevant:

> 
> So, we need:
> 
> A factory function for each dependency.
> A construction with Annotated for each dependency to avoid duplicating Depends if one dependency will be used in multiple path operations.
> A unique name for each construction with Annotated so as not to shadow the origin class name.
> A lru_cache decorator for each singleton.
> While manageable at first, this list grows quickly and spreads across your codebase — making maintenance more difficult.
> 
> And the most important, path operations depend on the concrete implementation, not the abstraction — violating the [Dependency inversion principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dependency_inversion_principle).
title: 📝 Add docs for IoC containers using Dishka as an example

[alv2017]: (1)
In general this is a good idea to add a documentation on integration of FastAPI and monitoring tools.
However, adding this info into the [Handling Errors tutorial](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/) is a very questionable decision:

- The goal of the error handling tutorial is to explain the error handling mechanism of FastAPI
- Adding a section on monitoring tools into the tutorial adds extra complexity and distraction.
- Monitoring and Observability tools is quite an extensive topic, that deserves its own tutorial(s).

(2)
I also recommend to focus on English version only, until you get an approval of your work from the maintainers.
title: 📝 Explain APM Services for Error Handling

다음과 같이 번역하였습니다.
- unwrap -> 언랩(unwrap) #13061 의 accept 여부에 따라 번역 변경 예정
- unwraping -> 언래핑(unwrapping) #13061 의 accept 여부에 따라 번역 변경 예정
- type annotation -> 타입 어노테이션(type annotation)
- argument value -> 인자 값(argument value)

text: Pull Request #12424: docs: update markdown formatting in zh-hant learn ## Summary I noticed that the `zh-hant` learn block is weird, Now it looks like the below image I made some changes so that it looks great
title: docs: update markdown formatting in zh-hant learn

text: Discussion on PR #12424 (docs: update markdown formatting in zh-hant learn): [github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit 8d599fd818acefe49f366f8bf221c048605d4687 at: https://aef4c3e2.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev ### Modified Pages * https://aef4c3e2.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev/zh-hant/learn/ [yychanlee]: @alejsdev please review this PR, it's just a little markdown error like the image below. It's already been fix to bold markdown. Thank you! Before: After: [mattwang44]: Please note that spaces should not be placed between Chinese characters, and I consider this PR to be a workaround. A space should not be required for such Markdown syntax (e.g. GitHub markdown renders \*\*bold\*\*and\*italic\*and\*\*bold\*\* as **bold**and*italic*and**bold**), so there may be an issue with Markdown renderer of the doc builder. If you search the index page of the FastAPI doc (`https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/zh-hant/`) on the [Wayback Machine](https://web.archive.org/), you'll find that the page was rendered correctly in older snapshots. The last snapshot that rendered well was taken on July 31st, 2024 ([link](https://web.archive.org/web/20240731061243/https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/zh-hant/#_2)). However, it appears to have started being corrupted in the next snapshot taken on August 6th, 2024 ([link](https://web.archive.org/web/20240806104830/https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/zh-hant/#_2)). - Snapshot of the index page on July 31st: ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8990db26-07b0-4e17-bd21-5c05d4a7fb08) - Snapshot of the index page on August 6th: ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3ec092ca-37a6-44ec-a8b7-2d9653e7b6c9) [jiawei-hong]: Hi @mattwang44 @codingjenny I agree that there can be no white space in Chinese. A short-term solution is to use the `` tag. I will start looking for the root cause in the next few days. Should I open an issue to track this issue? [mattwang44]: @jiawei-hong Could you check if this issue occurs when using MkDocs alone? If so, it might be more appropriate to open the issue in the MkDocs repository rather than FastAPI's. [jiawei-hong]: @mattwang44 I just made a completely new mkdocs to test I think it's some problem with fastapi nit: `mkdocs` version is `1.5.3` You can see my screenshot below ![Screenshot 2024-10-21 at 7 11 02 AM](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/52725732-4f19-493c-b3fc-9b3ff57dedd5) I used markdown syntax in test mkdocs ```markdown # Hello 你可以將其視為一本**書籍**或一門**課程** ``` [github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit dd77280658856e54ccb958df2348a02f48eb99a1 at: https://7a9f4b6f.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev ### Modified Pages * https://7a9f4b6f.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev/zh-hant/learn/

Hello @tiangolo,

Thank you for this awesome framework! 🙇🏻‍♂️

I believe that it would be great to have an explicit Python 3.12 support, so I'd like to suggest changes that:
  * enable testing for Python 3.12 on CI
  * update `classifiers` at `pyproject.toml`
  * fix deprecation warning for tutorials

Best regards!
title: ✨ Add support for Python 3.12

Pull Request #10535: 📝 Fix types in examples under `docs_src/extra_data_types`

Before, the type checker would complain when [adding/subtracting these values](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/1c25e2d8dc99331290e99e17814ec4d83adce725/docs_src/extra_data_types/tutorial001_py310.py#L17-L18), as they can be None.

I made the parameters `start_datetime`, `end_datetime`, and `process_after` non-optional. Then I moved the remaining keyword parameter `repeat_at` to the end.

The code block highlights in the docs do not need to be updated, no new lines are added or removed.

The related five tests are updated accordingly: `pytest tests/test_tutorial/test_extra_data_types -k=test_openapi_schema` succeeds.
title: 📝 Fix types in examples under `docs_src/extra_data_types`

Yapısı oldukça basit olduğundan öğrenmesi de nispeten basittir, tabii dökümantasyonu bazı noktalarda biraz teknikleşmeye başlıyor.

[ivan-abc]: ```suggestion
Вы можете объявить тип ответа, указав аннотацию **возвращаемого значения** для *функции операции пути (path operation function)*.
```

[ivan-abc]: ```suggestion
    * Если данные невалидны (например, отсутствует одно из полей), это означает, что код *вашего* приложения работает некорректно и функция возвращает не то, что вы ожидаете. В таком случае приложение вернет server error вместо того, чтобы отправить неправильные данные. Таким образом, вы и ваши пользователи можете быть уверены, что получите корректные данные в том виде, в котором они ожидаются.
```

[ivan-abc]: ```suggestion
* Добавьте **JSON схему** для ответа внутри *операции пути (path operation)* OpenAPI.
```

[ivan-abc]: ```suggestion
Допустим, вы хотите, чтобы ваша функция **возвращала словарь (dict)** или объект из базы данных, но при этом **объявите выходной тип как модель Pydantic**. Тогда именно указанная модель будет использована для автоматической документации, валидации и т.п. для объекта, который вы вернули (например, словаря или объекта из базы данных).
```

[ivan-abc]: ```suggestion
!!! note "Примечание"
```

[ivan-abc]: ```suggestion
!!! tip "Подсказка"
```

Pull Request #9425: ✨  Allow `None` as return type for bodiless responses

Fixes #9424 

If the return annotation of the route function is exactly `"None"`, return `None` for the typed annotation. This allows the status code `204 No Content` to be used.
title: ✨  Allow `None` as return type for bodiless responses

[fnep]: I tested this using `pip install git+https://github.com/hofrob/fastapi@fix-return-no-content-future-annotations` and it fixes my issue in #9723. :+1:

[hofrob]: @csbasil I rebased after you approved it.

Are there any plans on merging this PR?

[Kludex]: There's an alternative to this on https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5860.

[hofrob]: > There's an alternative to this on https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5860.

Yes, I thought it's not necessary to get the actual type in this case, since `None` is a reserved word anyway.

It might be a bit faster to compare the string further up in the stack, but that's probably negligible.

There may be other arguments about using one over the other.

[jc-louis]: @tiangolo can we get this merged? it will fix [this issue](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/12415) 🙏

[hofrob]: Rebased on top of current `master` branch. 3.14 will enable [deferred evaluation of annotations (PEP649)](https://peps.python.org/pep-0649/) by default.

[YuriiMotov]: @hofrob, please don't force-push after you opened PR for review. It makes it harder to review changes

[Kludex]: > @hofrob, please don't force-push after you opened PR for review. It makes it harder to review changes

It's a 28 lines PR, and it was created more than 2 years ago...

[YuriiMotov]: > It's a 28 lines PR, and it was created more than 2 years ago...

Yes, this PR is small and will not take a lot of time to review it again, but it would be much more convenient to review 1-2 additional commits instead of reviewing all the diff again.
In general, after force-pushing to branch all approvals should be reset and re-requested since it's impossible to see what was changed since then

[tiangolo]: Thanks! :coffee: 

I tweaked the implementation to not depend on a literal `"None"` string and instead handle the `NoneType` that was not being handled. I also tweaked the test to simplify it and use explicit stringified return types to not depend on the possibly changing behavior of `from __future__ import annotations`. This is now ready. :nerd_face: :rocket:

[YuriiMotov]: ```suggestion
    assert response.status_code == http.HTTPStatus.NO_CONTENT, response.text
    assert not response.content
```
How about adding this assertion?

[hofrob]: Thanks! added
title: ✨  Allow `None` as return type for bodiless responses

Pull Request #3080: Allow parameterized router subclasses in applications.

This allows developers to to provide custom router subclasses to their FastAPI applications for further customization.

Documentation for this feature added [here](https://6075131e18fa27238205260c--fastapi.netlify.app/advanced/custom-request-and-route/#custom-apirouter-class-in-an-application).

Closes #3079.
title: Allow parameterized router subclasses in applications.

text:
Pull Request #3032: ✨ Support Python internal description on Pydantic model's docstring

## Problem

Currently, on FastAPI endpoint functions we can document for OpenAPI description/Swagger and for internal Python purposes:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def useful():
    """My description.
    \f
    Internal documentation.
    """
    ...
```
On Swagger we'll have:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/113333626-f3008200-9322-11eb-9f9c-ca6ba5782d25.png)
 
When documenting models, it doesn't have the same behavior:
```python
from enum import Enum
from fastapi import FastAPI

class Banana(str, Enum):
    """My description.
    \f
    Internal documentation.
    """
    HAHA = "haha"
    HOHO = "hoho"

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def useful(banana: Banana):
    ...
```
On Swagger, we'll have:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/113333882-48d52a00-9323-11eb-8d0e-eef8cc965d04.png)
    
## Solution

The proposed _two lines of code_ on this PR, makes it possible to have the same behavior as the endpoint description:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/113334003-6e623380-9323-11eb-849d-750a85f7847c.png)

It follows the same idea as: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/10397ddc30ef9bb26e9247f04fe20ce3045081c4/fastapi/routing.py#L350

## Related issues
- https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/3024

## Questions & Alternatives
- Should Pydantic handle this on the [model_process_schema()](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/e68c277afe6bf6e7966bfecfe9b228d33f2de44b/pydantic/schema.py#L519) function instead?
title: ✨ Support Python internal description on Pydantic model's docstring

Pull Request #2036: ✨ Allow custom middlewares to raise `HTTPException`s and propagate them

When trying to build a custom middleware that checks content size any exceptions raised by the middleware don't get propagated back up. By allowing any HTTPExceptions to be raised as they are we can raise custom exception information into the response

![Screenshot 2020-09-11 at 18 20 03](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23500353/92891936-ad9a6700-f45b-11ea-8ea5-fc9c3d5193cf.png)
title: ✨ Allow custom middlewares to raise `HTTPException`s and propagate them

Pull Request #26: Add Open API prefix route - correct docs behind reverse proxy

I run a lot of services in Kubernetes using [Ambassador](https://www.getambassador.io/) as my API Gateway. Ambassador allows you to configure a route prefix for each one of your services and then it handles ingress and routing to the initial service. This PR enables me to configure the same prefix I use with Ambassador in the Swagger UI API docs.

Example:
```python
prefix='/service1'

app = FastAPI(
    openapi_prefix=prefix
)
@app.get('/something') => SwaggerRoute (GET - /service1/something)
```

NOTE: This is not the same as using the include_router method and adding a prefix.
This is meant only to rewrite the route paths in the Swagger UI.

BTW: Awesome Project! Thanks so much for your work on this, I'm using fastapi a ton these days.
title: Add Open API prefix route - correct docs behind reverse proxy
