Pull Request #15477: feat(deps): merge Body/Form/File/Query with Depends(model) in Annotated

### Example

```python

def register_post_route(
    path: str,
    schema: type[ItemBase],
):
    @router.post(path)
    def create_item(
        item: Annotated[
            ItemBase,  # for type checkers and IDEs
            Body(),  # for FastAPI - where to search
            Depends(schema),  # for FastAPI - what to expect
        ],
    ):
        return item

    return create_item
```

### Summary

FastAPI can combine `Body` / `Form` / `File` / `Query` with `Depends(some_model)` inside the same `Annotated[...]` so validation / OpenAPI use the runtime model while the parameter’s declared type stays the static first argument (e.g. base class for type checkers).

Order of `Body`/`Form` / … and `Depends` in `Annotated` does not matter.

### Motivation

Factories / dynamic routes need a non-literal model class; documenting and supporting `Annotated[Base, Body(), Depends(schema)]` avoids losing the location marker or the model.

### Implementation notes (high level)

New merge path in dependency / param handling (`fastapi/dependencies/utils.py`): detects compatible marker sets, validates constraints (e.g. no `Query` combined with body-like markers, not on path params, `Depends` resolves to a single `BaseModel` subclass, etc.), builds merged field info and annotation.

### Docs

New advanced section **“Composing Annotated metadata for runtime validation schemas”** with subpages (body / form / file / query), nav in `mkdocs.yml`, link from Request Body tutorial.

### Tests

- integration-style tests for body, file, form, query, and query+body shapes
- shared helpers / doc example loaders as staged under `tests/`.
title: feat(deps): merge Body/Form/File/Query with Depends(model) in Annotated

Pull Request #15187: fix: reject newline characters in SSE event and id fields

## Summary

`format_sse_event()` in `fastapi/sse.py` interpolated the `event` and `id` fields
into SSE wire-format bytes **without stripping or rejecting newline characters**.
A `\n` or `\r\n` in either field injects an additional SSE field-line into the
stream, enabling event-type spoofing and fabricated `data:` payloads in browser
`EventSource` clients.

The `data` and `comment` fields in the same function are correctly protected via
`splitlines()`. The omission for `event` and `id` is an inconsistency — not a
design decision.

**Vulnerability class:** SSE Protocol Injection (CRLF injection into wire format)
**CWE:** CWE-116 — Improper Encoding for Output in a Different Plaintext Context
**OWASP:** A03:2021 — Injection
**Prior art:** Hono [GHSA-p6xx-57qc-3wxr](https://github.com/honojs/hono/security/advisories/GHSA-p6xx-57qc-3wxr) — identical bug class, same fix pattern

## Inconsistency before this fix

| Field     | Newline handling         | Safe? |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------|
| `data`    | `splitlines()` loop      | ✅ Yes |
| `comment` | `splitlines()` loop      | ✅ Yes |
| `event`   | None                     | ❌ No  |
| `id`      | Only `\0` check          | ❌ No  |
| `retry`   | `int` type (no strings)  | ✅ Yes |

## What the injection looks like

A request like `GET /stream?type=chat%0Adata:%20{"cmd":"exec"}` causes
`format_sse_event` to produce:

```
event: chat
data: {"cmd":"exec"}
data: "real server data"

```

The browser's `EventSource` receives a `chat` event with a fabricated `data:`
field prepended to the real payload. Frontends that process `event.data`
line-by-line (common in streaming AI/MCP UIs) receive a corrupted payload.

## Changes

- **`fastapi/sse.py`**
  - Add `_check_event_no_newline()` validator; apply to `ServerSentEvent.event`
    via `AfterValidator` (mirrors the existing `_check_id_no_null` pattern)
  - Extend `_check_id_no_null()` to also reject `\n` and `\r`
  - Strip `\r`/`\n` from `event` and `id` in `format_sse_event()` as
    defense-in-depth (low-level function; callable without going through
    the Pydantic model validators)

- **`tests/test_sse.py`**
  - 7 new regression tests covering LF/CR rejection in `ServerSentEvent.event`,
    `ServerSentEvent.id`, and the `format_sse_event()` low-level function

## Test results

All 25 tests pass (`pytest tests/test_sse.py -v`), including the 7 new ones.

## Severity

**Low at framework level / Medium in affected applications.** The vulnerability
only activates when a developer routes attacker-influenced input into the `event=`
or `id=` fields — most correct uses pass static strings. The fix is a one-line
guard that prevents the framework from silently accepting malformed input.
title: fix: reject newline characters in SSE event and id fields

text: Discussion on PR #15187 (fix: reject newline characters in SSE event and id fields): [codspeed-hq[bot]]: ## Merging this PR will **not alter performance** `✅ 20` untouched benchmarks --- Comparing subhashdasyam:fix/sse-newline-injection (9e51a7d) with master (8238446)[^unexpected-base] [^unexpected-base]: No successful run was found on master (937d307) during the generation of this report, so 8238446 was used instead as the comparison base. There might be some changes unrelated to this pull request in this report. [subhashdasyam]: Hi maintainers 👋 The **Labels / check-labels** CI check is failing because this PR is missing one of the required labels. This is a security fix (SSE protocol injection via unvalidated `event` and `id` fields — CWE-116), so the appropriate label would be **`security`**. Could a maintainer please apply the `security` label so the check passes? Thank you! [subhashdasyam]: Fix tracked in issue #15188. [SadManFahIm]: Thanks for the thorough fix and the regression tests! A few observations while reviewing: **1. Bare `\r` test coverage** The validator at line 46 correctly rejects `\r` alone (`"\n" in v or "\r" in v`). Could you confirm whether `tests/test_sse.py` includes an explicit test case for a bare carriage return without a newline? ```python # e.g. with pytest.raises(ValueError): ServerSentEvent(event="chat\radmin_command") ``` **2. Inconsistency in `format_sse_event()` for null bytes on `id`** The `_check_id_no_null_or_newline` validator raises `ValueError` on `\0`, but `format_sse_event()` at line 220 silently strips `\0` via `.replace("\0", "")`. These two code paths have different behaviors for the same input — is that intentional? If the validator rejects null bytes at the model level, should `format_sse_event()` also raise (or at least not strip silently)? **3. `event` has no null-byte check** `id` has had null-byte protection since the original `_check_id_no_null`. The new `_check_event_no_newline` doesn't include a similar `\0` check for `event`. Is that an intentional asymmetry, or worth adding for consistency? Overall the fix looks solid — the two-layer approach (validator + defense-in-depth sanitization in `format_sse_event`) is a good pattern. Just flagging these for completeness.
title: fix: reject newline characters in SSE event and id fields

text:
Pull Request #13974: ⚡️ Cache callable inspection result

Currently FastAPI calls `inspect.signature`, `inspect.isgeneratorfunction`, `inspect.isasyncgenfunction`, `inspect.iscoroutinefunction`, etc for each callable found in dependency tree, and this is done for each received request.

This can be avoided by introducing a cache for methods result. As callable objects are usually created once and never change after that, this cache can be bound to endpoint or even whole application. Using WeakKeyDictionary helps to avoid memory leaks then callables are created in runtime (e.g. by using `functools.partial`).

This cache is different from dependency cache - dependency cache stores values of resolved dependencies (non reusable between requests), then callable info cache stores signatures and other information about code  (reusable between requests).

Before - `app (fastapi/routing.py)` takes 68.27% of all method calls:
![profile_proxy_with_auth_and_cache](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/40824f9d-2464-46be-a2ce-a89ce55bd274)

After - `app (fastapi/routing.py)` takes 54.52% of all method calls:
![profile_proxy_with_auth_and_cache_fastapi_inspect_patch](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cf7fc9e6-2a46-4fc1-b194-1f504f67b8aa)

The code is ugly as walking on dependency tree requires passing cache deep into dependency resolvers. Also lots of intermediate functions have default argument values, which are not used in most cases, but I have to follow this pattern.
title: ⚡️ Cache callable inspection result

text:
Discussion on PR #13974 (⚡️ Cache callable inspection result):

[dolfinus]: There is more performant approach though - to build dependency graph during application startup, not while receiving each request. If this is done only once, there is no need to cache signatures

[github-actions[bot]]: This pull request has a merge conflict that needs to be resolved.

[YuriiMotov]: @dolfinus, hello!

I attempted to test the performance impact (see code in the details), but I got a performance decline instead of increase..
What am I doing wrong?

<details>

```py
import asyncio
import time
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, Header, Query
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute
from typing import Optional


# -----------------------
# Dependencies
# -----------------------

# Sync dependency
def get_token_header(x_token: str = Header(...)) -> str:
    if x_token != "secret-token":
        raise ValueError("Invalid token")
    return x_token


# Async dependency
async def get_query_param(q: Optional[str] = Query(None)) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.001)  # simulate small async delay
    return q or "default"


# Nested dependency (sync)
def get_user(token: str = Depends(get_token_header)) -> dict:
    return {"username": "test_user", "token": token}


# Nested dependency (async)
async def get_full_context(
    user: dict = Depends(get_user),
    query: str = Depends(get_query_param),
) -> dict:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.001)
    return {"user": user, "query": query}


# -----------------------
# App & route definition
# -----------------------

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/items/{item_id}", dependencies=[Depends(get_token_header)])
async def read_item(
    item_id: int,
    context: dict = Depends(get_full_context),
) -> dict:
    return {"item_id": item_id, "context": context}


# -----------------------
# Performance test
# -----------------------

def test_get_route_handler_performance(loop_count: int = 1_000_000):
    route: APIRoute = app.routes[-1]
    assert route.path == '/items/{item_id}'

    start_time = time.perf_counter()

    for _ in range(loop_count):
        route.get_route_handler()

    duration = time.perf_counter() - start_time

    print(f"Called get_route_handler() {loop_count:,} times in {duration:.4f}s")
    print(f"Average per call: {duration / loop_count * 1e6:.2f} µs")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_get_route_handler_performance()

```

</details>

[dolfinus]: @YuriiMotov, thanks for the benchmark. There was one `iscoroutinefunction(call)` left behind, fixed. Now it's 1.96µs per call vs 2.61µs on master branch (on 50_000_000 iterations).

✨ Add support for enabling/disabling `input` and `url` fields in validation errors

text: Discussion on PR #13657 (✨ Add support for enabling/disabling `input` and `url` fields in validation errors): [kinuax]: I added docs and tests. [YuriiMotov]: For now the `ValidationError` schema is: It doesn't include any of these fields (just for completeness, `ctx` field is also missed in schema). IMO, this should definitely be fixed for `input` field (should be added as optional). Having this PR merged we will need to add optional `url` field as well. Not sure somebody needs `url` field. Are there any discussions about this? [kinuax]: Hi @YuriiMotov, many thanks for checking this PR out. I share a couple of discussions about this topic: -https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/10352 -https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/discussions/7485 Regarding the `url` field, I think it would be helpful for users who are aware of the underlying Pydantic layer and for cases where the error message is not fully clear, as it would provide a direct link to the relevant documentation. [bonastreyair]: will this ever be reviewed? I think it would help many people that want full control on the `input` or `url` of the schema validation errors when using FastAPI... @YuriiMotov @tiangolo 🙏 @kinuax maybe a rebase and adding that missing 1% of coverage will help, no? [YuriiMotov]: It's already possible to exclude `input` field from the error response by setting the exception handler for `RequestValidationError`. Adding `url` field is not trivial task, but I think this is very rare use case. This PR would bring some convenience for quite rare cases when people need to change this behavior, but it adds 2 additional parameters to `FastAPI()`. I think, if we add all possible configuration options that people request as parameters, we will end up having very large list of parameters and this will add extra complexity for people to navigate through all these parameters to find the one they need. So, we should only add those are commonly needed. Looking at the reactions here and on mentioned discussion, I can't say this feature is requested by many people. See also my previous comment: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/13657#issuecomment-3078177959 Having this, I think we should close this PR. I'll pass this to Sebastián, he will review and take a decision. @kinuax, thanks again for your efforts! [bonastreyair]: I understand, thanks for your quick response! What do you think about FastAPI dynamically using the attribute of the schema parameter defined within the pydantic model https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/api/config/#pydantic.config.ConfigDict.hide_input_in_errors, then we could make it transparent to FastAPI and then be completely configurable using pydantic schemas options. Would that make sense to have that easier UX?
title: ✨ Add support for enabling/disabling `input` and `url` fields in validation errors

text:
Pull Request #13265: 🌐 Add Korean translation for `docs/ko/docs/advanced/custom-response.md`

## 내용
이 PR은 `docs/ko/docs/advanced/custom-response.md` 파일에 대한 한국어 번역을 추가합니다. FastAPI 프로젝트에 두 번째로 기여하는 PR이며, 공식 문서를 기반으로 번역을 완료했습니다.

## 참고 사항
- 문서에서 **"Custom"**을 **"사용자 정의"**로 번역하여 맥락에 맞는 표현을 사용했습니다.
- 번역 작업은 최신 FastAPI 문서를 기반으로 진행되었으며, 원본의 Markdown 구조 및 코드 블록은 그대로 유지했습니다.

## 번역 기준
- 번역 과정에서 다음 용어를 사용했습니다:
  - **Custom**: 사용자 정의
  - **Response Class**: 응답 클래스
  - **HTMLResponse**: HTML 응답
  - **Media Type**: 미디어 타입

## 작업 이력
- 관련 커밋: `🌐 Add Korean translation for docs/ko/docs/advanced/custom-response.md`

## 기여 메시지
이 PR은 저의 두 번째 오픈소스 기여입니다. 번역 스타일이나 프로세스에서 부족한 점이 있다면 적극적으로 피드백을 반영하겠습니다.

## 검토 요청
- 번역 내용이 자연스럽고 명확한지 확인 부탁드립니다.
- **"Custom"**을 **"사용자 정의"**로 번역한 부분이 적절한지 검토 부탁드립니다.
- 문서 포맷이나 번역 스타일에서 개선이 필요한 부분에 대한 의견을 주시면 감사하겠습니다.

text:
Pull Request #12197: 🐛 Remove `Required` shadowing from fastapi using Pydantic v2

Re:
* https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/8212
* https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/8450
* https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/11952

(and potentially others)

I think I found the underlying cause. We're still supporting `pydantic.Required` in FastAPI, which has the possibility to shadow the new `Required` type. To me, this seems like a Pydantic + fastapi bug. Here's how 0.113.0 introduced it:

1. `fastapi` has the new `get_model_fields()` function, which deals with `TypeAdapter()`, and is called for every field
2. TypeAdapter gets a `globalns` from the `sys._getframe()`, meaning it gets the ns from `_compat`. This is important because pydantic bases their type evaluations from that `globalns`. Why they are doing this is a question for later.
3. Because `Required = PydanticUndefined` is in _compat.py, we are getting that value set in the `globalns`
4. Independently, `openai` uses `TypedDict` with the `Required` annotations in its fields.
4. `vllm` (and potentially other libs) uses `fastapi` + `pydantic` + `openai` TypedDict classes

The smoking gun was: `TypeError: 'pydantic_core._pydantic_core.PydanticUndefinedType' object is not subscriptable`

Focusing on https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/issues/8450, the offending type annotation was `Required[Union[str, Iterable[X]]`. It needed to have been `Required` that was a `PydanticUndefinedType`, otherwise it would be complaining about `Union[str, Iterable[X]]`, or some other annotation. I ran the snippet [here](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/12133#issuecomment-2342858880) using `fastapi 0.114.1, pydantic 2.9.0, vllm 0.6.1, python = 3.8.20`, and was able to reproduce the issue again. Using `pdb.pm()`, I was able to inspect the globalns and connect the dots.

# How to test

Run the code example in [this](https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/12133#issuecomment-2342858880) comment Python 3.8. It will fail without the changes, and pass with the changes.
title: 🐛 Remove `Required` shadowing from fastapi using Pydantic v2

[tiangolo]: Great job with all the detective work @pachewise! And thanks for the very clear explanation and references.

Thanks a lot @svlandeg for all your help and review! :bow: 

This will be available in the next hours in FastAPI `0.115.1` :rocket:

[pachewise]: not sure if we'd like to keep this or completely remove it

[pachewise]: These examples seemed old, and I don't see them in the published docs. Guidance wanted on whether these need to be updated, deleted, or left untouched.

[svlandeg]: This has been deleted from the (English) docs in PR #10469, but the Russian documentation does in fact still reference it in `docs/ru/tutorial/query-params-str-validations.md`. Ideally, we probably should remove the reference from the Russian docs as well, and then remove this file altogether, but I would suggest to do that in a separate PR to avoid mixing up different issues - it's always easier to review atomic PRs.

[svlandeg]: ```suggestion
    RequiredParam = PydanticUndefined
    Undefined = PydanticUndefined
```
This might be a matter of personal preference, but I would use binary assignments instead of tertiary ones. I also think it makes maintenance of code easier on the long run, as you can change one of the assignments and only that particular line will show up in the diff, rather than doing a mix of things on one line.

[svlandeg]: ```suggestion
```
title: 🐛 Remove `Required` shadowing from fastapi using Pydantic v2

Pull Request #11855: 📝 Update Gunicorn guide to use uvicorn-worker library

# Update FastAPI Server Workers Documentation

## Description
This PR updates the FastAPI Server Workers documentation to reflect the changes in Uvicorn worker implementation. The `uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker` has been [deprecated as of Uvicorn version 0.30.0](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/releases/tag/0.30.0), and we're now recommending the use of the `uvicorn-worker` package.

## Changes
- Updated installation instructions to include the `uvicorn-worker` package
- Changed references from `uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker` to `uvicorn_worker.UvicornWorker`
- Updated the explanation of the `--worker-class` option to reflect the new import

## Rationale
These changes are necessary to keep our documentation up-to-date with the latest best practices for deploying FastAPI applications with Gunicorn and Uvicorn workers.

## Testing
- [x] Verified that the installation instructions work with the latest versions of the packages
- [x] Tested the Gunicorn command with the new worker class
- [x] Ensured that the document flow and overall structure remain intact

## Additional Notes
Users upgrading from an older version may need to update their deployment scripts to use the new `uvicorn_worker.UvicornWorker` class. We may want to consider adding a migration guide or note in the documentation to help with this transition.
title: 📝 Update Gunicorn guide to use uvicorn-worker library

Pull Request #11339: 📝 Update OpenAPI client generation docs to use `@hey-api/openapi-ts`

Update openapi typescript client codegen docs. 

See `openapi-typescript-codegen` maintainers comment [here](https://github.com/ferdikoomen/openapi-typescript-codegen/issues/2064#issuecomment-2002593442) and [here](https://github.com/ferdikoomen/openapi-typescript-codegen/issues/1276#issuecomment-1302392146)

The [@hey-api/openapi-ts](https://github.com/hey-api/openapi-ts) repository was originally a fork, but has been detached as we plan to continue maintaining but also add improvements to the original project.

Many bugs in the original project have been fixed already in this package. We are actively tracking reported issues to the repository and will fix as quickly as possible.

Docs related:
- The command has been renamed from `openapi` to `openapi-ts` to avoid clashing.
- `useUnionTypes` was removed and is now default behaviour.
- `useOptions` is being deprecated and is now default behaviour with warning otherwise.

Disclaimer:
I am one of the people who is going to be maintaining the new package.
title: 📝 Update OpenAPI client generation docs to use `@hey-api/openapi-ts`

text:
Discussion on PR #10861 (✨ Add automatic OPTIONS request with route schema):

[whysage]: @kaze thank you for the request approval!

[lyksdu]: @whysage @kaze  Sorry to disturb. This merge request is a very meaningful merge request. Is there a way for us to rerun the two tasks that did not get results so that this request can be merged smoothly?

[whysage]: > @whysage @kaze Sorry to disturb. This merge request is a very meaningful merge request. Is there a way for us to rerun the two tasks that did not get results so that this request can be merged smoothly?

Hi @lyksdu, I tried to get review and approve for this pull request from Aug 10, 2023 to Mar 19, 2024 (7 month) and after all, was blocked from discussion (https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/2008#issuecomment-2007639569). Maybe it's fair due to many messages and tagging others.

If this pull request has any chance to be merged, I'll continue work on it.

[YuriiMotov]: @whysage, thanks for your interest! And thank you for your patience🙏

Could you please explain why we may need the support for `OPTIONS` method if we already have openapi schema that contains the information about the supported methods for each path? Please, add this information (and, probably, reasons to have this feature) to the description of this PR to simplify the review process

[whysage]: @YuriiMotov I could only quote the original issue text 
"I know I can get the information I need from /openapi.json, but that involves more processing in js and more data downloaded."
https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/2008#:~:text=body%20model(s).-,I%20know%20I%20can%20get%20the%20information%20I%20need%20from%20/openapi.json%2C%20but%20that%20involves%20more%20processing%20in%20js%20and%20more%20data%20downloaded.,-In%20the%20past

[YuriiMotov]: > @YuriiMotov I could only quote the original issue text "I know I can get the information I need from /openapi.json, but that involves more processing in js and more data downloaded." #2008

Yes, but:
* This PR doesn't implement the feature that is requested in that issue (it's requested to return the schema, not the list of supported methods)
* Say we need this to avoid loading and parsing openapi.json, how the client will get the list of supported paths?

Could you please explain it? I don't quite understand how it works.

I thought you can just [generate the client SDK](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/generate-clients/) automatically from openapi.json and then just use that SDK in your client code.

[whysage]: @YuriiMotov, thanks for the reply!
You are absolutely right. Currently, the PR introduces a standard OPTIONS response with "Allow" data containing a list of allowed HTTP methods.
I'm also not sure:
1) if it brings value now
2) if OPTIONS is designed to return URL schema.

Maybe we can tune the PR in the direction of handling preflighted requests in CORS https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Methods/OPTIONS#preflighted_requests_in_cors if this brings value and has a use case for it.

Thanks for the review!
title: ✨ Add automatic OPTIONS request with route schema

[dpinezich]: Würde ich geringfügig umschreiben, damit es einfacher verständlich ist: Standardmäßig liefert **FastAPI** die Rückgabewerte (Responses) als `JSONResponse` zurück und fügt den Inhalt der jeweiligen *Pfadoperation* in das `JSONResponse` Objekt ein.

[dpinezich]: statt "sie erstellt" => "werden diese Elemente erstellt", das vereinfacht das Lesen aus meiner Sicht.

[dpinezich]: Ich habe einen Versuch gewagt, diesen Text zu vereinfachen: Um dies zu erreichen, importieren Sie `JSONResponse`, und geben Sie Ihren Inhalt direkt zurück, indem Sie den gewünschten `status_code` setzen:

=> Ich finde das "Ihren Inhalt" eher schwer verständlich, aber ist im Englischen nicht anders, daher keine Anpassung :-).

Pull Request #9829: 📝 Clarify in CORS docs that wildcards and credentials are mutually exclusive

According to the Mozilla documentation. Wildcards cannot be used in conjunction with credentials in CORS requests. This is not limited to `allow_origins` but it covers most of the `allow_*` See: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/CORS#credentialed_requests_and_wildcards

text:
Pull Request #9299: Add FromLifespan to extract dependencies from the lifespan state

This adds a `FromLifespan` annotation that extracts dependencies from lifespans, which I believe satisfies the requests in #617, #9215, part of #3641 and probably several other issues.

It also adds what is essentially an extractor system that allows dependencies to get a view into their parameter; this could effectively allow replacing a lot of `fastapi/dependencies/utils.py` with implementations for each type of parameter, making things like #2077 a lot easier to implement and opening up the door for 3rd party extensions to FastAPI's dependency / extractor system. This does the rest of #3641.

I purposefully added as single docs-style test to demonstrate how this is used and in-lieu of writing docs; I'll do that once / if there's some initial feedback and buy in.
title: Add FromLifespan to extract dependencies from the lifespan state

text:

[github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit 7f2bf1e518f4530b13806408f0ae09ba7d7b4fe8 at: https://641be2efb8c906675eb00d9c--fastapi.netlify.app

[Kludex]: I'm failing to see how all those issues are related to this. :thinking: 

Don't we want a dependency that can be accessed on the lifespan itself instead? Like the "app" scope that you have on xpresso.

If I do the following, isn't it the same as what this PR proposes?
```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, Request
from contextlib import asynccontexmanager
from typing import Annotation

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI) -> dict:
    async with connect() as db:
        yield {"db": db}

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

async def get_connection(request: Request):
    yield request.state.db

@app.get("/")
async def homepage(db: Annotation[Session, Depends(get_connection)]):
    ...
```

[adriangb]: Yes, it's the same thing. This is just making it more seamless. It’s the same with the rest of the dependency injection system: you could just not use it and store stuff in `request.state` instead. There’s pros and cons, but I think that’s not a discussion for this PR.

Minor nit: it would be `request[“state”][“db”] I think.

[sm-Fifteen]: @adriangb: I'll echo what Kludex and Chippiewill are saying here, I'm not sure that's the... FastAPI-tonic?? ...way of handling lifetime-scoped DI. I get the benefits you're bringing up with regards to a "composition root" as opposed to dependencies being spread out everywhere, but consider how all of FastAPI is designed around being spread out like that, on account of [using routing annotations](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/687) instead of a single routing table. Single roots make sense for Starlette, but I'm not so sure it's the path I'd want FastAPI to go down if we want the experience to be consistant.

My suggestion in #617 was more or less exactly what you ended up implementing in xpresso, with the intended goal that I could have an Oracle connection pool, a Postgres connection pool, an HTTPX connection pool and a... anything that isn't a connection pool, all declared in either the same or different files as needed, without the need for a single lifespan function to tie them together when initializing my FastAPI app, making their usage mostly seamless when compared to current dependencies.

[adriangb]: Thanks @sm-Fifteen the feedback is helpful. I’ll try to cook up a version of this that uses that approach.

[YuriiMotov]: @adriangb, thanks for your efforts!
Are you still interested in working on this?
I think we need to ask Sebastián to confirm the idea of this feature first
title: Add FromLifespan to extract dependencies from the lifespan state

text:
Pull Request #5433: Allow a _least privilege_ model of automatic dependencies by "disabling" dependencies for individulal routers or endpoints.

Issue #2481 Mentions how it is difficult to remove a global dependency on a subset of endpoint.  The proposed solution in that defect is to add global depdencies on individual `APIRouter` instances and combine a set of those into the app.

This PR proposes a complimentary solution.  Anywhere where a `Depends(foo)` or `Security(bar)` is added as an item in a `dependencies` keyword argument, it can receive a `disable=True` keyword argument.

Once the final parameterless dependencies are gathered and added on a route, these are then processes and collapsed.  This allows an `APIRouter` to disable a global dependency which has already been added on an app object.

This allows a *least privilege* approach to API design, where nothing is allowed by default unless explicitly allowed on a case by case basis:  The `app` object can have a global dependency which requires **all** scopes, and then individual endpoints, for example those that deal with authentication, or other less privileged actions, can remove the more stringent scopes to allow access.

Additionally, multiple stacked calls to the same `Security` dependency are collapsed, merging all the provided *scopes* into a single list.  This allows the creation of virtual scopes, for example, prefixing a scope with a "-" do disable it, handling the processing of the all the scopes to a single call.  This is yet-another way disabling a scope requirement.

This is currently an undocumented feature request / proposal, containing tests.
title: Allow a _least privilege_ model of automatic dependencies by "disabling" dependencies for individulal routers or endpoints.

text: Pull Request #5354: ✨ Generate automatic language names for docs translations This PR does two things: 1) Update the code that generates the mkdocs.yml files to always include a local language name The local language name is read from a new file which contains mappings for all the allocated ISO 639-1 language codes, and the content of that file is sourced from [https://quickref.me/iso-639-1](https://quickref.me/iso-639-1), though with updates to persist the existing language names where there was a difference with the existing name for the language in the FastAPI source. ⚠️ The old code always put English at the top of the language list in the UI, but the new code doesn't | Before | After | | ------------- | ------------- | | | | 2) Regenerate all the mkdocs.yaml files

text:
Pull Request #5023: ✨ Add `SimpleJSONResponse` for decimal JSON encoding

As mentioned in [#4998](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/4998#issuecomment-1150242750). I believe `simplejson` is the best option to accomplish high decimal points real number encoding, hence the need of `SimpleJSONResponse`. I followed some of the parameters used in `starlette`'s original `JSONResponse` [here](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/starlette/responses.py#L195-L201) to make sure the output are the most consistent as possible.

If interested, I'm also willing to draft a page of doc detailing the best practice of handling decimal in FastAPI I've come up so far. Just let me know if that's something you'd like to include.
title: ✨ Add `SimpleJSONResponse` for decimal JSON encoding

I understand there's nothing on OpenAPI specifying the order that parameters should have in the schema related to the parameters in the path string, they are supposed to be matched by name, not by position/order. I'm also thinking if there's a different way to achieve this.

For now, it seems there are some conflicts and tests not passing, would you be able to fix those?

text:
Pull Request #3305: 🛠 Native JWT support

### Relevance
About half of the modern internet uses raw JWT (the other half uses OAuth2, which is already supported).  
I think that in such a modern library as `fastapi`, native support of JWT will be a big advantage.

### Existed solutions 
Only 1 external lib: [github:fastapi-jwt-auth (192 stars)](https://github.com/IndominusByte/fastapi-jwt-auth/) - an flask-jwt-auth customization. 

The following drawbacks are significant:
1. poorly supported (last updated in 2020)
2. depends on the original library
3. DOES NOT match the `fastapi` security style (`Security`/`Depends` function args wrapper)
4. no `openapi` specification  generation

### Existed Issues and Pull Requests:
* Searching 'jwt' or 'json web tokens' - 'No results matched' (except docs example)

---

### Features 

- [x] bearer/cookie token place
- [x] access/refresh tokens 
- [x] time expiring tokens 
- [x] `auto_error=False` support
- [x] `openapi` specification generation
- [ ] fresh token demand
- [ ] token revocation
- [ ] cookies name selecting
- [ ] docs examples (after approval)


### Requrements 
`python-jose` for tokens interaction and `pytest-mock` for testing time-dependent functions.
 

### Usage example 
All available use cases can be found in the test files. The simplest example is provided below:

```python
app = FastAPI()

access_security = JwtAccessBearer(secret_key="secret_key")

@app.post("/auth")
def auth():
    subject = {"username": "username", "role": "user"}
    access_token = access_security.create_access_token(subject=subject)
    return {"access_token": access_token, "refresh_token": None}

@app.get("/users/me")
def read_current_user(credentials: JwtAuthorizationCredentials = Security(access_security)):
    return {"username": credentials["username"], "role": credentials["role"]}
```

---

Any suggestions for improvement are gladly discussed!
title: 🛠 Native JWT support

text:
Discussion on PR #3305 (🛠 Native JWT support):

[acutaia]: @k4black  in security docs @tiangolo  in the past used  Pyjwt but later he moved to [python-jose](https://github.com/mpdavis/python-jose)  PR  [#1610] so I think we should consider using python-jose for your pull request 🤔

[k4black]: @acutaia 
I saw this warning in docs, however, there were no arguments in favour of a younger library. Initially I used `python-jose` 😃, but migrate to `PyJWT`, as the most popular library according to [jwt.io :libraries:python](https://jwt.io/).  
Although I agree that to comply with the general style, there may be a need to rewrite it with `python-jose`. I'll work on it

[k4black]: @tiangolo Can you approve or decline this PR, please

[jakemanger]: Is this going to be approved or has this just been ignored? Seems like a nice, easy way to add authentication with the option of refresh tokens to me.

[JanBeelte]: Is there any chance that this PR or comparable functionality will be merged soon?
What are the next steps here @tiangolo @cikay @k4black @acutaia , I might be willing to take over some work.
Would be very handy to have jwt support out of the box!

[and-sm]: @tiangolo, any update?

[jpmckinney]: python-jose is unmaintained. Please do not use it.

[nsteinmetz]: > python-jose is unmaintained. Please do not use it.

latest version of fastapi-jwt allows to use authlib instead of python-jose (still present as fallback)

[Pandede]: Any update?

[tiangolo]: Thanks for the interest @k4black! 

So, JWT and OAuth2 are two separate things. OAuth2 is about the exchange of communication via HTTP to handle auth stuff and exchange of tokens, without specifying any format for the tokens.

JWT is a format for the tokens. Using OAuth2 it's quite common to exchange JWT tokens.

"Bearer" is a header format to transfer tokens. Normally, the 3 go together.

For example, if you check the security docs, they are about using Bearer tokens in JWT format using OAuth2.

Parsing and handling tokens is not really dependent of any web stuff, so I think it would be better in an external package.

This also adds a strong dependency on python-jose that I wouldn't want to take right now.

For now, I'll pass on this one, but thanks for the interest! :cake:

[LiorA1]: I got here because I'm new to FastAPI, but not sure in my implementation of the access & refresh tokens..
Maybe its worth to include in the documentation, because its a common practice

[cikay]: Do not reverse the condition add the raise keyword to the if block.

[cikay]: This is where you should reverse the condition the success case should be at the bottom. [It is called early return or fast fail.](https://szymonkrajewski.pl/why-should-you-return-early/)
title: 🛠 Native JWT support

Pull Request #112: Static swagger

About #110 

it adds an optional `static_directory` to FastAPI, if it's None (which it is by default) the generated html uses the cdn existing at present and the icon also.
If it's set to a path, path is added as a starlette StaticFile, and in that case, FastAPI needs an extra dictionary with js, css and favicon

I'd rather discuss if you're ok with that implementation before doing /redoc :see_no_evil: , some stuff might be moved in `setup()`

~and I'm struggling with those mypy errors, would love some help !~
title: Static swagger

Pull Request #14964: 🗑️ Deprecate `ORJSONResponse` and `UJSONResponse`

🗑️ Deprecate `ORJSONResponse` and `UJSONResponse`

Now that there's better performance by default, with response models: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14962

This removes `ujson` and `orjson` from the `"fastapi[all]"` extras.

To use these responses, `ujson` or `orjson` need to be explicitly installed.
title: 🗑️ Deprecate `ORJSONResponse` and `UJSONResponse`

text: Discussion on PR #14964 (🗑️ Deprecate `ORJSONResponse` and `UJSONResponse`): [github-actions[bot]]: ## 📝 Docs preview Last commit c21b0aac6d9eb3b240941f51d42735e912b2f87f at: https://e3d39a97.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev ### Modified Pages * https://e3d39a97.fastapitiangolo.pages.dev/reference/responses/ - ([before](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/reference/responses/)) [codspeed-hq[bot]]: ## Merging this PR will **not alter performance** `✅ 20` untouched benchmarks --- Comparing deprecate-orjson-ujson (c21b0aa) with master (2e62fb1) [remimd]: With this deprecation, what's the recommended approach for exception handlers that return plain dict bodies? For example: ```python @app.exception_handler(ValidationError) async def validation_error_handler(request: Request, exception: ValidationError) -> Response: return ORJSONResponse( status_code=status.HTTP_422_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY, content={"errors": exception.errors()}, ) ``` Since exception handlers don't use Pydantic return types, they don't benefit from the Pydantic serialization. Should we just switch to JSONResponse and accept the performance difference, or is there another approach? [tmdgusya]: Are there any benchmark or something? [YuriiMotov]: > With this deprecation, what's the recommended approach for exception handlers that return plain dict bodies? You can copy the implementation of `ORJSONResponse` and use it instead of importing from `fastapi`. Or, you can return `Response(content=ujson.dumps(content, ensure_ascii=False).encode("utf-8"))`. Don't forget to add `ujson` explicit dependency to your project [eltoder]: > Are there any benchmark or something? @tmdgusya I ran benchmarks for my responses (https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/14299#issuecomment-3493559629). Feel free to plug in yours and check. [remimd]: @YuriiMotov thanks for the answer! Though I think deprecating these classes is a mistake, for plain dict serialization, orjson are still significantly faster than stdlib, and that's free performance. Copying the implementation feels like a workaround for removing something that's still useful.
title: 🗑️ Deprecate `ORJSONResponse` and `UJSONResponse`

Pull Request #14805: 🐛 Fix `get_model_fields` to support Annotated types

#14482 added the model_config to the ModelField to support arbitrary types, however the if statement did not check for all the correct types.

This PR brings the check inline with the internal pydantic TypeAdapter check which would throw an error in some cases, e.g. Union fields with single Values.

Can you come up with a more realistic code example? Ideally something that we can not do in a simpler way [ilovemesomeramen]: @YuriiMotov What do you mean exactly? The provided example is exactly the issue which this PR solved, this example worked in 0.124.0 but does not in 0.124.1 A little bit of context why this is needed: I often design my APIs in such a way that it easy to extend the models in the future. So if i know a specific model will be discriminated in the future i want the OpenAPI spec to reflect that immediately. the provided test case is based on this test: tests/test_arbitrary_types.py The Problem is the Union with a single value in it this worked before and no longer works since the type is not properly extracted. Pull Request #14262: ✨ Add support for dependencies with scopes, support `scope="request"` for dependencies with `yield` that exit before the response is sent

✨ Add support for dependencies with scopes, support `scope="request"` for dependencies with `yield` that exit before the response is sent

This adds support for

* `Depends(func, scope="request")`, the default.
* `Depends(func, scope="function")`, early exit, after the function, but before sending the response back.

---

I'm considering an alternative name for the `scope` parameter of `mode`, to avoid any confusion with OAuth scopes, e.g. `Security(scopes=["blah"])`, which is a completely different idea.

Or a potential future `Depends(oauth_scopes=["blah"])` to replace `Security(scopes=["blah"])`.

That's the only reason to consider `mode` over `scope`.

---

I'm also considering the values, currently they are:

* `"function"`: start before the *path operation function*, exit after the function returns but **before** the response is sent.
* `"request"`: start before the *path operation function* (same as above), exit **after** the response is sent back.

I'm considering an alternative name for `"request"` of `"response"`, with the same behavior.

The rationale for using `"request"` as the value is that the dependency runs around (start and end) of the *request* cycle.

The rationale for using `"response"` as the value is that the main difference with `"function"` is when the exit code is run, in `"function"` it is run *after* the function is done, in this second form (`"request"` or `"response"`) it is run *after* the *response* is sent back.

---

I'm using this interface design because I also plan on (potentially, possibly) add a new `scope="lifespan"` that would allow using dependencies (or equivalent) functions that are run only once per app, and closed when the app ends. This way the same functionality for dependencies could be used for the app lifespan (ASGI `startup` and `shutdown` events). It should be more intuitive for users to work with. But these are future plans.

---

### 2025-10-03 Edit

After asking in several places, I concluded to keep the parameter name as `scope`.

I'll also keep the current values of `"function"`, similar to pytest, and `"request"`, to avoid confusing users who could think that the dependency will start running when the response is being sent, but it starts when the request starts.
title: ✨ Add support for dependencies with scopes, support `scope="request"` for dependencies with `yield` that exit before the response is sent

text:
Discussion on PR #14262 (✨ Add support for dependencies with scopes, support `scope="request"` for dependencies with `yield` that exit before the response is sent):

[potiuk]: Small comment - I am not sure how well I can comment on the code itself :) as I am vaguely familiar with it, but I think I can comment on the UX of it. 

* scope is way better choice than mode, more natural. And I think there is very little probability of people confusing `Dependency(scope=` with `Security(scope=`. Having both as keyword parameters and practically always being specified right after the class, namespaces them properly already. It very nicely reads as "dependency_scope" and "security_scope" - following nicely "literate programming" principles by Donald Knuth.

* I think maybe a better and more consistent naming for scopes would be "request" and "response" - where request would be an "early" exit, and "response" would be "late" exit after response is prepared. This is - I think way more natural and describes the expected behaviour way more intuitively for my taste. Having "function" and "request/response" as two scopes somehow mixes the abstraction layers and it's not too intuitive. 

UPDATE: comment - I have not spent a lot of time with FastAPI so my suggestions might be missing some of the context, this is mostly based on my general programming intuition.

[Danstiv]: Hello!

Let's consider the following pseudo-example:

```python
def db_dep():
    # some code to create and yield a sqlalchemy session
    ...

def chat_repo_dep(db_session = Depends(db_dep)):
    # some code to create a chat repository
    ...

@router.post("/")
def create_chat(repo: ChatRepository = Depends(chat_repo_dep)):
    ...

@router.get("/")
def get_chat(repo: ChatRepository = Depends(chat_repo_dep)):
    ...

@router.patch("/")
def update_chat(repo: ChatRepository = Depends(chat_repo_dep)):
    ...

@router.post("/generate")
def generate_llm_response(repo: ChatRepository = Depends(chat_repo_dep)) -> StreamingResponse:
    # some code that returns a StreamingResponse
    ...
```

So, we have a dependency with a SQLAlchemy session, and also a chat repository dependency that requires the SQLAlchemy session.

We have many regular endpoints that return a response all at once, and the behavior for them is fine.
However, some endpoints might return a `StreamingResponse`, and the response iterator won't be able to work with the chat repository because it depends on the DB session, which was closed when `generate_llm_response` returned the `StreamingResponse`.

Also, we cannot influence this behavior when declaring the dependency, since `generate_llm_response` depends on the chat repository, not on `db_session` directly.

The only solution I've come to is to create a separate repository for `StreamingResponse` and use it in the iterator.

Therefore, it might be worth considering something like a `StreamingResponse` with dependencies.
title: ✨ Add support for dependencies with scopes, support `scope="request"` for dependencies with `yield` that exit before the response is sent

text:
Pull Request #14232: ♻️ Simplify code in `fastapi/routing.py`

TLDR: just simplify internal logic and code a bit, doesn't change framework behaviour


split in in 4 commits:
1) remove unused variable initialization `errors: List[Any] = []`


2) simplify code: remove nested code, using early exit pattern

```
if errors:
  raise Exception()
```


3) instead of:
```
current_status_code = (
    status_code if status_code else solved_result.response.status_code
)
if current_status_code is not None:
    response_args["status_code"] = current_status_code
if solved_result.response.status_code:
    response_args["status_code"] = solved_result.response.status_code
```

write:
```
if solved_result.response.status_code:
    response_args["status_code"] = solved_result.response.status_code
elif status_code is not None:
    response_args["status_code"] = status_code
```

if remains exactly the old behaviour, but 
a) looks more clear (right now looks like `status_code if status_code else solved_result.response.status_code` is default value, but actually in the end default value is `solved_result.response.status_code`
b) it doesn't make any speed effect, but new version use less comparison and assignment operations


4) remove non used variable `response` initialization
title: ♻️ Simplify code in `fastapi/routing.py`

```
typer scripts/docs.py run add-permalinks
```

Re-generate permalinks for all En docs:

```
typer scripts/docs.py run add-permalinks --update-existing
```

Add missing permalinks to specific page in En docs:
```
typer scripts/docs.py run add-permalinks-page docs/en/docs/advanced/behind-a-proxy.md
```

But instead of removing it, I would go with `f"reference{os.sep}",`

[YuriiMotov]: Looked at code and realized that there are a lot of other occurrences of hardcoded `/`.
So, we need to decide whether we want to refactor it in this PR on in separate PR?

[svlandeg]: I suggest we (only) update the hardcoded slashes of the new code that is contributed with this PR. Because when I run this new script on my Windows machine, it starts generating permalinks for the `reference` docs and we don't want to do deal with any PRs of users that get the same thing.

With respect to hardcoded slashes already in the current code base: let's edit those in a separate PR.

[svlandeg]: > But instead of removing it, I would go with f"reference{os.sep}",

That's fine too by me!

[YuriiMotov]: Done!
title: 🛠️ Add `add-permalinks` and `add-permalinks-page` to `scripts/docs.py`

Pull Request #13803: 🐛 Improve `jsonable_encoder` for other types of iterables

## What was changed
Updated the jsonable_encoder function to correctly handle any iterable type.

Reordered conditionals to ensure the iterable check occurs later, allowing appropriate handling to other type-specific branches (which previously caused failures).

## Why this change is necessary
The previous contribution at #30 did not (fully) serialize objects like `sqlalchemy.engine.Row`, which inherit from `collections.abc.Sequence` but behave differently than standard lists or tuples. This led to unexpected failures or incorrect encoding.

## Testing
Manually tested the updated function against a wide variety of data structures
Behavior appears consistent and correct across all tested inputs.

## Notes
While the function now gracefully handles many iterables, I'm still wary there may be edge cases I haven't considered. Further review and test cases are welcome.
title: 🐛 Improve `jsonable_encoder` for other types of iterables

Pull Request #13765: 📝 Clarify that OpenIdConnect does not perform token validation

This PR updates the docstring of the `OpenIdConnect` class to clarify that:

- It does **not** perform token validation or decoding.
- It only extracts the `Authorization` header and integrates with OpenAPI docs.
- Developers must implement actual authentication logic separately.

This change aims to prevent confusion and reduce the risk of unintentional misuse in production environments.
title: 📝 Clarify that OpenIdConnect does not perform token validation

Pull Request #13207: 🐛 Fix OpenAPI schema support for computed fields when using `separate_input_output_schemas=False`

This demonstrates how the output schema differs when the FastAPI app runs with different values for `separate_input_output_schemas` - namely, the computed fields are omitted.

Initially reported here: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/10342
title: 🐛 Fix OpenAPI schema support for computed fields when using `separate_input_output_schemas=False`

text:
Discussion on PR #13207 (🐛 Fix OpenAPI schema support for computed fields when using `separate_input_output_schemas=False`):

[svlandeg]: Thanks! We'll look into this and get back to you 🙏  

(I'll put this PR in draft while we work on it)

[vgrafe]: Hi all. Is it possible to have another look into this?

[svlandeg]: @vgrafe: apologies for the delay. Yes, I'll look into this in the next few weeks.

[svlandeg]: Ok, so I looked into this and @ldthorne was right that the issue occurs at https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/073e7fc950f1af24634b304afbb34bf212756dff/fastapi/_compat.py#L186-L188

Specifically, when `separate_input_output_schemas` is `False`, this `override_mode` will set all fields to `"validation"` mode. However, to preserve computed fields, they should remain in `"serialization"` mode. The thing is, we can't just change this override mode, as this would prevent the unification of input/output schemas we'd expect from setting `separate_input_output_schemas` to `False`.

So, I'm finding it to be a bit of a philosophical question as to what `separate_input_output_schemas=False` actually means. If it means that the input and the output schema should (always?) be **_identical_**, then really we should be excluding any generated fields, in which case we don't need this PR and it's not a bug but a feature 😬

[svlandeg]: @vgrafe: for me to better understand your use-case: why are you not able to use `separate_input_output_schemas=True`?

[svlandeg]: Update: OK, so I ended up trying to implement a solution that would keep the unifying behaviour, while still adding extra computed fields if there are any. The tests succeed, but we definitely need a few more additional tests to cover various edge cases (and to ensure the coverage test succeeds).

I'm still not 100% sure we want to go down this route though, so I'll first wait and sollicit feedback from either @tiangolo, @YuriiMotov or another maintainer before I continue here.

[YuriiMotov]: The behavior I would expect here is: force separate schema creation if model contains computed field.

So

https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/_compat.py#L197-L199

Should be something like:
```py
        override_mode: Union[Literal["validation"], None] = (
            None if (separate_input_output_schemas or has_computed_fields(field)) else "validation"
        )
```

And the meaning of `separate_input_output_schemas=False` could be described as: do not create separate input\output schemas if not mandatory

[svlandeg]: > The behavior I would expect here is: force separate schema creation if model contains computed field.

Thanks for the review! That is basically the same behaviour as what I had previously implemented, but using your feedback I've now pushed an updated implementation that is much cleaner and easier to maintain / understand.

[github-actions[bot]]: This pull request has a merge conflict that needs to be resolved.
title: 🐛 Fix OpenAPI schema support for computed fields when using `separate_input_output_schemas=False`

Pull Request #12955: ✨ Add support for ReDoc parameters

This PR add simple support for ReDoc parameters - see here https://redocly.com/docs/redoc/config

Example config:
```python
{
   "disable-search": "true"
}
```

Will look like this:

```python
app = FastAPI(
    title="My API",
    docs_url=None,
    redoc_url="/docs",
    openapi_url="/docs/openapi.json",
    redoc_ui_parameters={
        "disable-search": "true"
    }
)
```

Will be added to the generated html:
```html
<redoc spec-url="/docs/openapi.json" disable-search="true"></redoc>
```

ReDoc will pickup the parameter and act accordingly.
title: ✨ Add support for ReDoc parameters

[maliayas]: Great PR. I'd love to see it merged soon. Currently there is no way to customize ReDoc.

https://github.com/hasansezertasan/openapipages

Alright. In the meantime I incorporated your feedback. Thank you very much.

[nidhishgajjar]: **Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM 5.1 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Review of PR #12955: Add support for ReDoc parameters

Nice feature addition — bringing ReDoc customization in line with the existing `swagger_ui_parameters` support. The pattern is consistent and the implementation is straightforward.

### What looks good ✅

- **Consistent API**: Follows the same pattern as `swagger_ui_parameters` — Dict parameter on `FastAPI.__init__`, passed through to HTML generation
- **Tests included**: Both with and without parameters cases tested
- **Docstring**: Mentions kebab-case convention which matches ReDoc's API
- **Minimal change**: Clean, focused implementation with no unnecessary changes

### Security observation 🟡

The HTML attribute injection doesn't sanitize values:
```python
for key, value in redoc_ui_parameters.items():
    config_string += f' {key}="{value}"'
```

While this is developer-controlled config (not user input), a value containing `"` could break out of the attribute. For robustness, consider escaping:
```python
from html import escape
config_string += f' {escape(key)}="{escape(str(value))}"'
```

This is low risk since it's server-side config, but `swagger_ui_parameters` avoids this issue by using `JSON.stringify()` in JavaScript. Since ReDoc uses HTML attributes, explicit escaping would be a safer approach.

### Value type handling 💡

F-string conversion means `True` becomes `"True"` (capital T) but ReDoc expects `"true"` (lowercase). Consider converting booleans explicitly:
```python
if isinstance(value, bool):
    value = str(value).lower()
```

Or document that values should be pre-converted to strings.

### Summary

Solid feature addition that addresses a real gap in ReDoc customization. The HTML attribute injection pattern would benefit from value escaping and boolean handling, but the overall approach is clean and consistent with FastAPI's existing patterns.

**Assessment: comment** (HTML attribute escaping, boolean value handling)
title: ✨ Add support for ReDoc parameters

text:
Pull Request #12455: ✨ Add schema generator class param to FastAPI

This feature allows setting a custom JSON schema generator at app-level, in order to customize JSON schema from pydantic-core schema generation as described in the following pydantic guide:

https://docs.pydantic.dev/2.1/usage/json_schema/#customizing-the-json-schema-generation-process

:warning: This feature is only available when using pydantic V2. Setting the additional parameter while using pydantic V1 will not have any effect.

For more context, the schema `Literal["something"]` generates a schema with both `enum` and `const`, which can be traced back to [literal_schema in pydantic/json_schema.py](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/18d39febf00cb07954e19ebbdbd8168ef20e3df1/pydantic/json_schema.py#L736) brought by pydantic [commit 18d39febf00cb07954e19ebbdbd8168ef20e3df1](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/commit/18d39febf00cb07954e19ebbdbd8168ef20e3df1). It was asked from my team to either present `const` or `enum`, but not both, as it raises errors in some schema validation processes, which we can't do unless we can override the JSON schema generator, as described in the guide above, hence this pull request.
title: ✨ Add schema generator class param to FastAPI

text:
Discussion on PR #12455 (✨ Add schema generator class param to FastAPI):

[thomas-touhey]: I did not take pydantic v1 into account apparently. Will look into this in more detail later today (French time).

**EDIT**: Nevermind, finished it in the end.

[thomas-touhey]: Hi @tiangolo @alejsdev, any chance I could have review or merge on this PR please? :)

[thomas-touhey]: Still up for some review on this PR! :)

[thomas-touhey]: _(just rebasing on master to ensure the PR is still mergeable, still waiting for some review)_

[YuriiMotov]: @thomas-touhey, thanks for your interest in FastAPI!

There were several similar PRs and they were closed due to weak reasoning.

You already provided a use case, but maybe we can elaborate on this and add more cases?
For example, I saw people wanted to change items sorting order in openapi (or avoid sorting them), and as far as I understand it can be achieved using this feature.

Let's bring all use-cases together and add them to PR's description!

Also, since it only works with Pydantic V2, I think we should mention this in the docstring and in the description of the PR.
I would even show a warning message if this parameter is specified with Pydantic V1

[thomas-touhey]: Hi @YuriiMotov, sorry for the late reply, at this point I wasn't expecting any answer on this PR and I was quite busy for the last weeks. ^^

I don't really have more cases myself than the one provided, the reasoning was mostly "if pydantic fucks up some checks / compatibility from other tools also using OpenAPI specs (such as the one I mentioned), this could be a quick and accessible way to fix it without having to fork FastAPI altogether".

I'm not working at the company I was when this PR was useful, and the issue I was trying to resolve stayed stale: since the API gateway we were using could not validate the app's schema, for some time the team fixed the schema produced by FastAPI manually, then for quicker releases the team ended up abanding the API gateway for the frontend to hit the API directly. The situation was not very pretty, but it was either that or having to maintain a FastAPI fork with that PR merged, which would have taken too much work AFAIK; all things considered, not the best situation. But what else can you do when a PR that passes CI checks doesn't receive answers for8 months...

I haven't encountered the other use cases you have seen, so I prefer not to guarantee their legitimacy.

I'll add the Pydantic V2 warning in the PR's description. If you have remarks on the actual implementation, feel free to make them so I can fix them, otherwise I don't really see what else I can do on the matter :)

Pull Request #12028: 🌐 Update Chinese translation for `docs/zh/docs/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield.md`

https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/pull/10831 ensures that the code following `yield` executes after the response is create but before it is sent to the client. The statement in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/issues/3620#issuecomment-888081460 was outdated, it is now safe to commit the session after `yield` as the code is guaranteed to execute after the response is created but before it is sent.
```python
def get_db() -> Session:
    session = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield session
        session.commit()
    except:
        session.rollback()
        raise
    finally:
        session.close()
```

text:
Pull Request #11160: 🐛 Fix root_path behavior

**Description**

I came across a bug with setting the `root_path` value directly into the `FastAPI` app. The docs state:

> Passing the `root_path` to `FastAPI` would be the equivalent of passing the `--root-path` command line option to Uvicorn or Hypercorn.

But that wasn't the case since providing the `--root-path` option to Uvicorn or Hypercorn makes it so that the scope's `path` and `raw_path` contain the `root_path`. The behavior in FastAPI was to patch the scope's `root_path` value only, leaving the `path` and `raw_path` unchanged.

When running behind a proxy, this would cause the 307 redirect on missing trailing slashes to set the `Location` header to the path without the `root_path` value, resulting in a 404 error.
title: 🐛 Fix root_path behavior

text:
Discussion on PR #11160 (🐛 Fix root_path behavior):

[insistence]: @tiangolo Excuse me, would it be convenient for you to prioritize this PR?

[jmmk]: I ran into the same thing after upgrading FastAPI.

Setup:

Reverse Proxy at `/api` that strips the `/api` prefix
FastAPI with `root_path="/api"`
Router with prefix like `/my-router` and a handler at `/`, creating a route like `/my-router/`

Symptoms:

A request to `/api/my-router` returns a 307 redirect to `/my-router/` which will hit our reverse proxy with `/my-router/` and match nothing or whatever happens to be configured at that path
A request to `/api/api/my-router` (with an extra `/api` in there) returns a 307 redirect to `/api/my-router/` which matches
A request to `/api/my-router/` will match the handler and return the expected response

[dolfinus]: > Reverse Proxy at /api that strips the /api prefix

You can setup reverse proxy to not strip that prefix, and run FastAPI with `--root-path=/api`

[wynneevans]: This is issue is also affecting my team. We wanted to update from pydantic v1 to the latest v2 version. When updating pydantic from v1 to v2 and using FastAPI in the same project, pip updates FastAPI accordingly. In our cases that was 0.95.1 to 0.112.0. This issue was a horrible gotcha because we had to update pydantic model code during this upgrade, so it was natural to suspect pydantic not fastapi was causing the error somehow. Now we have isolated the issue to updating fastapi from 0.108.0 to 0.109.0. We have pydantic 2.8.2 installed.

def create_app() -> FastAPI:
    """Create service app (FastAPI object)"""
 
    logger.info("Initializing web application...")
    get_configs(Path(__file__).parent.parent / 'config_env.ini', config_type='env')
    app = FastAPI(root_path='/index-analytics/profgen')   #  this line only works with fastapi up to v0.108.0
    #app = FastAPI(root_path='')   #  this line when uncommented works with fastapi v0.109.0
    app.mount('/v0', v0.create_app())  #  v0.create_app() involves the proxy
 
    @app.get('/echo')
    def echo() -> dict:
        return {'message': 'success'}
 
    return app

Would this pull request allow the line containing "root_path='index-analytics/profgen'" to work with fastapi v0.109.0+ ?

[Soufian20]: Any updates on that?

[kunesj]: I can confirm that the bug this fixes still exists in FastAPI 0.115.12.
In my case, it is causing 404 errors when accessing mounted `StaticFiles`. I hope it can be merged soon.

[github-actions[bot]]: As this PR has been waiting for the original user for a while but seems to be inactive, it's now going to be closed. But if there's anyone interested, feel free to create a new PR.

[richin13]: It took them 18 months to review this and the bot closed it in less than 1 month 😂
title: 🐛 Fix root_path behavior

text:
Pull Request #10694: ✨ Add support for using `@app.api_route()` in user code with multiple `methods`

See details in discussion: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/8449

When creating an API endpoint with more than one method, the unique id is being generated for only the first method.
The proposed solution keeps the current functionality for regular endpoints (with only one method) and regenerates the unique id for each method separately.

Example:

```python
@app.api_route("/multiple-methods", methods=["GET", "POST"])
def multiple_methods():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
```

will generate 2 unique IDs:
- `multiple_methods_multiple_methods_get`
- `multiple_methods_multiple_methods_post`

instead of generating `multiple_methods_multiple_methods_post` twice.
title: ✨ Add support for using `@app.api_route()` in user code with multiple `methods`

text:
Discussion on PR #10694 (✨ Add support for using `@app.api_route()` in user code with multiple `methods`):

[dagadbm]: can someone please merge this?

I am trying to source control openapi-schema.json and do a git diff when it changes but I can't since it always changes the operationId randomly.

Is there something that can be done user side to prevent this?

[dagadbm]: You know what i ended up doing?

Just removing all methods and writing each one separately.

Also had to sort the json schem api dump and now I finally have an idempotent openapi schema generator function which we now source control which we can also use to generate typescript types

[Acervans]: Any reviewer to resolve the PR please? It's been well over a year since its creation

[ogunoz]: Any chance it will be merged?

[YuriiMotov]: This PR also fixes one more thing.

The following test fails **sometimes** in current version (0.115.12) because schema name may be either `Body_update_item_items__item_id__put` or `Body_update_item_items__item_id__patch`:

```python
from fastapi import Body, FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel


def test_():
    app = FastAPI()

    class Item(BaseModel):
        name: str

    async def update_item(item_id: str, item_data: Item = Body(embed=True)):
        pass

    app.add_api_route("/items/{item_id}", update_item, methods=["PATCH", "PUT"])

    openapi = app.openapi()

    assert openapi["paths"]["/items/{item_id}"]["put"]["requestBody"] == {
                    "required": True,
                    "content": {
                        "application/json": {
                            "schema": {
                                "$ref": "#/components/schemas/Body_update_item_items__item_id__put"
                            }
                        }
                    },
                }
```

This must be annoying for those who are using code generators.

With this PR schema name is always `Body_update_item_items__item_id_` for path operations with multiple methods.
title: ✨ Add support for using `@app.api_route()` in user code with multiple `methods`

text:
Pull Request #9928: 🐛 Fix validation error Optional[list[...]], Union[list[...], ...], Optional[set[...]] and etc in FormData

## Fix issues with `Optional` and `Union` for `list, set, tuple, etc...` in `Form`:

```python3
a: Optional[list[int]] = Form(None)
b: Union[list[int], list[float]] = Form(None)
```

which lead to a validation error: `"Input should be a valid list"`



## Reproduce the issue:

```python3
from typing import Optional
from fastapi import FastAPI, Form
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/form")
async def test_form(
    list_int: Optional[list[int]] = Form(None),
):
    return {"elements": list_int}


client = TestClient(app=app)


def test_send_optional_one_element():
    data = {"list_int": "100"}
    response = client.post("/form", data=data)
    assert response.json() == {"elements": [100]}

    # {'detail': [{'input': '100',
    #              'loc': ['body', 'list_int'],
    #              'msg': 'Input should be a valid list',
    #              'type': 'list_type',
    #              'url': 'https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.0.3/v/list_type'}]} != {'elements': [100]}


def test_send_optional_two_list_elements():
    data = {"list_int": ["10", "20"]}
    response = client.post("/form", data=data)
    assert response.json() == {"elements": [10, 20]}
    # {'detail': [{'input': '20',
    #              'loc': ['body', 'list_int'],
    #              'msg': 'Input should be a valid list',
    #              'type': 'list_type',
    #              'url': 'https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.0.3/v/list_type'}]} != {'elements': [10, 20]}


def test_send_optional_list_elements():
    response = client.post("/form")
    assert response.json() == {"elements": []}
    # {'elements': None} != {'elements': []}


```

Previously, the ModelField had a `shape` attribute that said what kind of field it was (list, set, tuple, etc.).
So then the `Optional[list[int]]` shape had `SHAPE_LIST`

In the new version, this field (shape) no longer exists, and for the Form to parse data, FastAPI itself tries to collect values depending on the type.
for `list[int]` iterates through the collection `value=12&value=20` and does `value=[12, 20]`

In the new version, `Optional[list[int]]` does not count as a list when tested with is_sequence_field , which causes the value to be fetched as one element by :
`value = received_body.get(field.alias)`

If we made an annotation like `Optional[list[int]]` then pydantic expects us to pass `[1, 2, 3]` or `[1]`, but we are passing the value directly, which causes a validation error
`"input should be a valid list"`

In simple terms, what is happening:
```python3
class Foo(BaseModel):
      a: Optional[list[int]] = Field(None)

Foo(a="1")  # raise ValidationError
```
And it should happen:
```python3
Foo(a=["1"])  # Ok
```

UPD:
Initially, I decided to add field_annotation_is_optional_sequence for both V1 and V2, but after analyzing in more detail, V1 works correctly because of the shape, so field_annotation_is_optional_sequence is needed only for V2.
title: 🐛 Fix validation error Optional[list[...]], Union[list[...], ...], Optional[set[...]] and etc in FormData

text:
Discussion on PR #9928 (🐛 Fix validation error Optional[list[...]], Union[list[...], ...], Optional[set[...]] and etc in FormData):

[dotX12]: @tiangolo, opened again, because the problem has not been fixed by anyone for a long time, please take a look :)

[dotX12]: @tiangolo Is there any information on when this will be approved and go to the master branch?
I, like many other people, cannot move to >0.100 because of this problem and continue to sit at <0.99 version.

[dotX12]: @Kludex, can we engage you to solve this problem? I understand that there are a lot of problems, but the fix is important and has not moved for half a year :(

[YuriiMotov]: Just noticed that optional list parameters are not displayed correctly in Swagger.
`list_int: Optional[list[int]] = Form(None)` is displayed as optional field without type. Even though in `openapi.json` its type is `null or array of integer items`.
This problem is also relevant to `Query` parameters.
So, I think the problem I've just mentioned should be solved separately from this PR. I'm going to create a new issue.

UPD: found similar question in discussions. It's proposed to use `list_int: list[int] | SkipJsonSchema[None] = Form(None)` to make Swagger understood parameter's type correctly. Ugly, but it works..

[zawsq]: Any updates to this?

As mentioned above `list_int: list[int] | SkipJsonSchema[None] = Form(None)` doesn't seem to work anymore, Swagger just refuse to display null value.

[dotX12]: @tiangolo, should we expect that this PR will someday end up in master?
Soon it will be a year since the problem in V2 exists.

[Danipulok]: Good day, @tiangolo!
Just today has this bug and found this issue.
Could you please tell us if this PR's planning to be merged? It's really useful and fixes some nasty bugs

[jiahuei]: This fix would be very helpful

[YuriiMotov]: Seems that this has been already fixed.
With FastAPI 0.115.6 only `test_send_optional_list_elements` fails, but I'm not sure that test itself is correct

[tiangolo]: It seems this was fixed in some recent version, so I'll close this one now. Thanks for the interest! :cake:

[dolfinus]: Why checking only first argument instead iterating all of them? Currently `Union[list, None]` will return True, but `Union[None, list]` not.

[fcrozetta]: ```suggestion
Você pode defini-lo com lógica de *inicialização* e *desligamento* usando os parâmetros de `lifespan` da aplicação `FastAPI`, e um "gerenciador de contexto" (Te mostrarei o que é isso a seguir).
```

[fcrozetta]: The last phrase is written in first person, and I'm not sure this is a good idea, but I'm keeping it like this to match the english version

[LorhanSohaky]: ```suggestion
Você pode definir a lógica (código) que poderia ser executada antes da aplicação **inicializar**. Isso significa que esse código será executado **uma vez**, **antes** da aplicação **começar a receber requisições**.
```

[LorhanSohaky]: ```suggestion
Do mesmo modo, você pode definir a lógica (código) que será executada quando a aplicação estiver sendo **encerrada**. Nesse caso, este código será executado **uma vez**, **depois** de ter possivelmente tratado **várias requisições**.
```

[LorhanSohaky]: ```suggestion
Por conta desse código ser executado antes da aplicação **começar** a receber requisições, e logo após **terminar** de lidar com as requisições, ele cobre toda a **vida útil** (_lifespan_) da aplicação (o termo "vida útil" será importante em um segundo 😉).
```

[LorhanSohaky]: ```suggestion
Pode ser muito útil para configurar **recursos** que você precisa usar por toda aplicação, e que são **compartilhados** entre as requisições, e/ou que você precisa **limpar** depois. Por exemplo, o pool de uma conexão com o banco de dados ou carregamento de um modelo compartilhado de aprendizado de máquina (_machine learning_).
```

text:
Pull Request #5897: feat: add custom route on route level

fixed: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1174 https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/4437
The problem:
Sometimes it is nice to have the ability to pass a custom route to a specific endpoint. For example, I need to check headers before processing with the body, [but FASTAPI forces me to read the body before.](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/fb7ea03bc728ea6528194b2a5a245d4ea9f3b0ff/fastapi/routing.py#L190)

Currently, I can't do it because `route_class_override` uses only internally, and need to reimplement a lot of things to solve it. So, my proposal is to let routers override a route class.


_I've tried to solve this issue through middlewares but it's not clear to check into middlewares if I should "filter" requests or not based on the path. Another solution is Mount, but it also not solving the problem because it requires creating sub-path for it (the same with `include_router`)._

PS:
I think my PR partially add support for that one(logically) https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/1649 https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/1464
title: feat: add custom route on route level

[github-actions[bot]]: 📝 Docs preview for commit 814d25639461479ce3785e158f7657489ef41468 at: https://63c8157edcd0ea06de59aa16--fastapi.netlify.app

[yezz123]: This really looks cool 🚀 

But if you see it from a different perspective, is that it may lead to routing conflicts if the custom routes overlap with previously defined routes.

This can lead to unexpected behavior and make the API more difficult to understand and maintain. Additionally, if the custom routes are not well-designed or validated, they can introduce security vulnerabilities.

[arkadybag]: @yezz123 That is the same as define custom route https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/custom-request-and-route/#create-a-custom-gziprequest-class.
The difference is only that you can use it on endpoint-based level. Your thoughts about 
> not well-designed or validated, they can introduce security vulnerabilities.

the same as for the custom route class and that is from a different scope. As a user I want to have an ability to overwrite it for endpoint, not for router.
